component_layouts.layout_narrative: The time-slot-list appears immediately below the date-selector and time-slot-filter-control in the left booking column, acting as the main interactive list. It spans the full width of the 8-column left area, with a generous top margin (24–32px) to clearly separate it from the filter row. The list is presented as a vertically scrolling set of cards or pill-shaped buttons, with each row having ample vertical padding (at least 12–16px between time options) to echo the spacious, low-density tone of the mockup. The visible portion of the list is constrained to a moderate height (e.g., 280–360px) so it does not visually overwhelm the page; if there are many slots, the list area itself can scroll internally. The left edge of the list aligns perfectly with the left edge of the date-selector, anchoring the visual flow. On smaller screens, it still sits directly under the date and filter controls, occupying full width.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrollable content block within the left column; the list section itself may have its own vertical scroll if many slots exist, while the page scrolls normally.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The participants-selector sits below the time-slot-list in the left booking column, acting as a compact but important step before the main booking form. It is aligned with the same left edge as the date and time list and occupies roughly half to two-thirds of the 8-column width, leaving breathing room on the right side. There is a 24–32px gap above it so it feels like a separate micro-section, but its vertical footprint is relatively small (a single row of controls with a label and plus/minus or dropdown). This positioning keeps the page’s vertical rhythm gentle: hero, date, times, then participant count prior to the more detailed form. On mobile, it stretches to full width and remains stacked directly beneath the time-slot-list.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline control within the left booking column, scrolling with the rest of the page content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The booking-form is located directly beneath the participants-selector in the left 8-column booking area, forming the final major segment of the left column. It spans nearly the full width of this column (leaving a modest right margin for comfort), and includes multiple vertically stacked fields for participant details and contact information. There is a generous top margin of about 32px from the participants-selector to signal a transition to a more detailed data-entry phase. Fields are grouped into logical clusters with 16–24px vertical spacing between groups, and labels are left-aligned so the layout feels consistent with the rest of the page’s linear, editorial style. On smaller devices, the form stretches to full width and maintains the same vertical spacing to avoid density creep.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrollable form section anchored in the left column; no special sticky behavior, but its vertical height may exceed the viewport, encouraging natural downward scrolling.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The booking-summary-inline sits in the right-hand column of the booking section, directly to the right of the date/time/participants/form stack. It shares the same 4 out of 12 grid columns as the coach-info-panel above, visually continuing the narrow vertical band established in the hero row. The summary card is aligned to the top of the booking section (roughly level with the top of the date-selector) and has 24–32px of internal padding, presenting consultation title, selected date/time, participants, price, and primary call-to-action. Its height is moderate, extending down roughly through half to two-thirds of the visible booking controls, and it remains visually separated from the left column by a consistent gutter or subtle vertical divider. On desktop, it is sticky: as the user scrolls through the booking-form, the summary card stays fixed within the viewport, pinned a comfortable distance (e.g., 80–96px) below the header, reinforcing the mockup’s right-hand guidance band. On smaller screens, the booking-summary-inline moves below the form, full width, losing its sticky behavior but keeping generous spacing above and below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sticky side-panel within the right booking column on larger screens (sticking within the viewport while the left column scrolls), reverting to a standard inline card stacked after the form on smaller devices.

name: Programs Listing
title: Programs
description: Browse and filter multi-week online programs, using dynamic filters for category, duration, price, rating, and sorting.

core_features: Load dynamic filter options (categories, available durations, rating thresholds, sort options) from getProgramFilterOptions and render them in filter controls.
Initialize filters and sort order from incoming URL parameters (category, duration_weeks, max_price, min_rating, sort) and apply them to the initial search.
Search active programs via searchPrograms using the current filter set, sort option, and pagination parameters.
Display a paginated list of programs with name, category, duration, delivery format, base price, rating, and rating count.
Allow users to filter programs by category, duration in weeks, maximum price, and minimum rating, updating the URL and results dynamically.
Allow users to sort programs by options returned from getProgramFilterOptions (e.g., rating, price, popularity), with the selected sort reflected in the URL.
Provide a way to change pages (next/previous or specific page), with the page number encoded in the URL and passed to searchPrograms.
Allow users to open a specific program detail page (program_detail.html) by clicking on a program card, passing program_id via URL.
Provide navigation to the shopping cart (cart.html) via a cart icon or link on the page.
user_workflows:
Workflow: Open Programs page from navigation
1. User clicks the 'Programs' link in the site header.
2. Browser loads programs.html without query parameters (or with parameters if coming from another page).
3. Page calls getProgramFilterOptions with no arguments to load available categories, durations, rating thresholds, and sort options.
4. Page reads current URL parameters (category, duration_weeks, max_price, min_rating, sort, page, page_size if present).
5. Page validates URL parameters against options from getProgramFilterOptions where applicable (e.g., category and sort).
6. Page derives the initial filter state from valid URL parameters (falling back to no filter or default options if parameters are missing or invalid).
7. Page calls searchPrograms with the derived filters, sort option, and page/page_size.
8. Page renders the program list, current filters, and pagination controls based on the searchPrograms response.

Workflow: User filters by category and duration
1. User opens programs.html (possibly with preselected category and duration_weeks from index.html).
2. Filters panel shows available categories and durations from getProgramFilterOptions, with any matching URL parameters preselected.
3. User selects or changes a category and a duration in weeks.
4. Filter component updates the URL query string to include category and duration_weeks (and preserve other existing parameters such as max_price, min_rating, sort, page_size) while resetting page to 1.
5. After the URL is updated, the page triggers a new call to searchPrograms using the updated filters and current sort option.
6. Program list updates to show only programs matching the selected category and duration.
7. Applied filters summary (if present) reflects the chosen category and duration, allowing the user to remove them, which again updates URL and re-runs searchPrograms.

Workflow: User filters by maximum price and minimum rating
1. User adjusts the maximum price control (slider or numeric input) to a new value.
2. On change or blur (depending on design), the page updates the max_price parameter in the URL (and keeps other existing parameters) and resets page to 1.
3. Page calls searchPrograms with the new max_price value included in filters.
4. Program results are updated to include only programs whose price is less than or equal to the specified max_price.
5. User selects a minimum rating threshold from rating options loaded via getProgramFilterOptions.
6. Page updates min_rating in the URL, resets page to 1, and calls searchPrograms with both max_price and min_rating in the filters.
7. Program list updates accordingly to show only programs with rating greater than or equal to min_rating.

Workflow: User changes sort order
1. User views current programs with some filters applied.
2. User opens the sort dropdown populated from getProgramFilterOptions.sort_options.
3. User selects a new sort option.
4. Page updates the sort parameter in the URL query string, resets page to 1, and triggers a new call to searchPrograms with the same filters but updated sort argument.
5. Program list re-renders in the new order as returned by searchPrograms.

Workflow: User uses pagination
1. User is viewing search results with filters and sort applied.
2. Based on searchPrograms.total, page renders pagination controls for the current page searchPrograms.page and page_size.
3. User clicks 'Next', 'Previous', or a specific page number.
4. Page updates the page parameter (and optionally page_size if user can change it) in the URL query string, preserving other filter and sort parameters.
5. Page calls searchPrograms with the same filters and sort but updated page/page_size values.
6. Program list updates to show results for the selected page.

Workflow: User navigates to a program detail page
1. User finds a program of interest in the programs list.
2. User clicks anywhere designated as clickable on the program card (e.g., the title or the entire card surface).
3. Click handler reads the program_id from the searchPrograms result item and navigates to program_detail.html?program_id=<program_id>.
4. Browser loads the program detail page, which can use program_id to load detailed information (handled on that page).

Workflow: User goes to cart from programs page
1. User is browsing programs.
2. User clicks the cart icon in the header or a 'View Cart' action on the page.
3. Browser navigates to cart.html (no additional parameters required for navigation).
interactions: On page load, call getProgramFilterOptions, then parse URL parameters to initialize filters, sort, and pagination, then call searchPrograms to populate the initial program listing.
When the user selects a category, update the category parameter in the URL (or remove it if cleared), reset page to 1, and call searchPrograms with the new filters.
When the user selects a duration in weeks, update the duration_weeks parameter in the URL (or remove if cleared), reset page to 1, and call searchPrograms.
When the user sets or changes the maximum price via slider or numeric input, update max_price in the URL, reset page to 1, and call searchPrograms.
When the user chooses a minimum rating threshold from the rating filter, update min_rating in the URL, reset page to 1, and call searchPrograms.

When the user resets all filters (via a 'Clear filters' action), remove category, duration_weeks, max_price, and min_rating parameters from the URL, reset page to 1, and call searchPrograms with an empty filters object.
When the user changes the sort option, update sort in the URL, reset page to 1, and call searchPrograms with the same filters and new sort.
When the user changes page through pagination controls, update page (and possibly page_size) in the URL and call searchPrograms with unchanged filters and sort but the updated pagination values.
When the user clicks a program card, navigate to program_detail.html with program_id set to the card’s program_id.
When the user clicks the cart icon or 'View Cart' link, navigate to cart.html.
When the URL changes due to back/forward browser navigation, re-parse the URL parameters and re-run searchPrograms with the reconstructed state so the view matches the URL.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page’s state is fully derived from URL query parameters and API responses. On initialization, the page calls getProgramFilterOptions to obtain dynamic options for categories, durations, rating thresholds, and sorting. After receiving these options, the page reads the current URL query parameters: category, duration_weeks, max_price, min_rating, sort, and optionally page and page_size. For parameters that correspond to constrained option sets (category, min_rating thresholds, sort), the page validates that the URL values exist within the sets returned by getProgramFilterOptions; invalid values are ignored. For numeric filters (duration_weeks, max_price) and pagination (page, page_size), the page parses them as numbers, ignoring values that cannot be parsed or that are non-positive. The effective filter state is then constructed as an object passed to searchPrograms: filters = { category, duration_weeks, max_price, min_rating } where only defined/valid keys are included. The sort parameter is passed as a separate argument to searchPrograms. Pagination state is passed via page and page_size. The response from searchPrograms (results, total, page, page_size) is the single source of truth for which programs are displayed and how pagination is rendered. Any user interaction that changes filter, sort, or pagination state first updates the URL query string (e.g., using pushState/replaceState) so that the state is encoded in the URL. After URL update, the page reconstructs state from the URL again and calls searchPrograms with that state. Browser back/forward navigation is handled by listening to URL changes and re-running the same reconstruction and search. No state is stored in localStorage; all persistent navigable state lives in the URL and is recalculated from the combination of URL and API responses.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial data loading and subsequent state synchronization between URL, filters, and results. On page load or on URL change, it calls getProgramFilterOptions, parses URL parameters, constructs filters and sort arguments, and then calls searchPrograms. It also provides a shared state or callbacks to child components for triggering URL updates and re-executing searches.

Provides interactive controls to adjust program filters (category, duration in weeks, maximum price, minimum rating). It consumes option data from getProgramFilterOptions and current filter state from URL-derived state. When the user changes any filter, it triggers an update to the URL query parameters (via callbacks to the initializer) and causes searchPrograms to be called with updated filters.

Renders a category dropdown or list using categories returned by getProgramFilterOptions. The currently selected value is derived from the category URL parameter when valid. On change, it updates the category parameter in the URL and initiates a new search via searchPrograms.

Renders a duration selector using durations_weeks from getProgramFilterOptions. When the user selects a duration (e.g., to see only 4-week programs), it updates duration_weeks in the URL, resets page to 1, and triggers searchPrograms so that results only include programs with matching duration_weeks.

Provides a control (slider or numeric input) where the user can set a maximum price. It reads the current value from the max_price URL parameter if present. On change or confirmation, it updates max_price in the URL (or removes it if cleared), resets page to 1, and triggers searchPrograms so that only programs with base_price less than or equal to this value are returned.

Displays minimum rating options based on rating_thresholds returned by getProgramFilterOptions. The selected value is derived from the min_rating URL parameter if it matches one of the thresholds. On change, it updates min_rating in the URL, resets page to 1, and triggers searchPrograms with the updated filter so only programs with rating greater than or equal to the threshold are returned.

Summarizes currently active filters (category, duration_weeks, max_price, min_rating) based on URL parameters. Each active filter is shown as a removable chip or item. When a filter chip is removed, the corresponding URL parameter is removed, the page is reset to 1, and searchPrograms is called with the remaining filters.

Shows sort options retrieved from getProgramFilterOptions.sort_options. The selected sort option is derived from the sort URL parameter if it matches one of the available options; otherwise it can default to a defined option (for example the first in the list). When the user selects a sort option, it updates the sort parameter in the URL, resets page to 1, and triggers searchPrograms with the same filters and new sort argument.

Displays meta-information about the current search results using the searchPrograms response: total number of matching programs and the current range shown based on page and page_size. It updates automatically whenever searchPrograms returns new data.

Renders the list of program cards using the results array from searchPrograms. Each item shows key Program-related attributes (name, category, duration_weeks, delivery_format, base_price, rating, rating_count). It updates whenever searchPrograms results change. It also handles click interactions on each program card to navigate to the program_detail.html page.

Represents a single program within the list. It receives a single result object from searchPrograms (program_id, name, category, duration_weeks, delivery_format, base_price, rating, rating_count). On click, it reads program_id and navigates to program_detail.html?program_id=<program_id>.

Uses searchPrograms.total, searchPrograms.page, and searchPrograms.page_size to render pagination controls (next/previous and/or page numbers). It reads the current page and page_size from URL parameters if present. When a user selects a different page or page size, it updates the page (and page_size if adjustable) parameters in the URL and triggers searchPrograms with unchanged filters and sort but updated pagination.

Displays a message or guidance when searchPrograms returns an empty results array while indicating that filters may be too restrictive. It reacts to searchPrograms responses and is shown only when total is zero. It can provide an action to clear filters, which removes filter parameters from the URL and re-runs searchPrograms.

Handles and displays errors if getProgramFilterOptions or searchPrograms fail (e.g., network errors). It provides a retry action which re-calls the failing interface with the current URL-derived state.
Provides a clickable icon or link that navigates directly to cart.html when activated. This component does not interact with cart data directly; it only manages navigation.

layout.grid_system: Visually consistent with a 12-column responsive grid centered in the viewport, with a max-width content container and substantial left/right margins.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows a vertical, top-to-bottom structure inspired by the mockup: a full-width utility bar and fixed header (logo left, centered navigation with the cart link on the right edge of the nav row), then a centered main content column within a 12‑column grid. Inside the main content, the programs area is treated as a single dominant, card-like module. At the top of this module sits a horizontal control band (results summary on the left, sort on the right), followed by a full-width applied-filters bar. Below that, the content splits into a two-column interior: a left filters sidebar and a right programs grid. All of this sits inside a visually unified card that echoes the hero card feel from the mockup.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows a vertical, top-to-bottom structure inspired by the mockup: a full-width utility bar and fixed header (logo left, centered navigation with the cart link on the right edge of the nav row), then a centered main content column within a 12‑column grid. Inside the main content, the programs area is treated as a single dominant, card-like module. At the top of this module sits a horizontal control band (results summary on the left, sort on the right), followed by a full-width applied-filters bar. Below that, the content splits into a two-column interior: a left filters sidebar and a right programs grid. All of this sits inside a visually unified card that echoes the hero card feel from the mockup.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The global header stack (utility bar + main header) occupies a shallow but clear band at the top of the viewport. The main programs card sits centrally and uses about 70–80% of the overall page width, leaving generous lateral margins. Inside that card, the top control band (results + sort + applied filters) uses roughly the top 20–25% of the module’s height in the first fold, with the remaining space dedicated to the filters sidebar and programs grid. Horizontally, the filters sidebar takes about 30–35% of the internal width, while the programs list takes 65–70%. Vertical spacing between the main structural bands (header, control band, filters/list, pagination) follows the generous 48–64px rhythm from the mockup, with 24–32px padding inside each card-like block.
layout_strategies.content_density: The page maintains a low, editorial-like content density. Controls are grouped into clear bands with ample white space, avoiding visual clutter. The filters in the sidebar are stacked with generous breathing room between groups, and the programs grid uses moderate card spacing so that each card can breathe. Only essential text is exposed at each hierarchy level (summary counts, key filter chips, card titles and key metadata), supporting the professional, corporate feel while staying approachable for a wellbeing/sports audience.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow mirrors the F-shaped pattern of the mockup. The user’s eye starts at the top left logo, moves across the centered navigation and cart link, then drops to the dominant programs card in the main content. Within that card, attention first hits the top-left results summary, then scans right to the sort control. From there, the eye moves down to the applied-filters bar, then continues down into the programs grid on the right. The filters sidebar to the left is discovered as a secondary vertical path as users scan down the page. Pagination and any state indicators sit at the lower right of the main module, completing the F-pattern and hinting at further content below.

layout.overall_description: Just below the fixed site header, a spacious, centered content container spans roughly 70–80% of the viewport width, framed by generous white margins on both sides. Inside this container, a single, large card-like module dominates the main view: a subtly elevated panel with a calm, light background and possibly a restrained blue gradient accent along one edge, echoing the bold hero tile in the mockup. At the top of this module, a horizontal band shows the programs results count and context on the left, while a clean sort dropdown is aligned on the right, giving a clear, corporate control strip. Immediately beneath, a full-width applied-filters bar presents active filter chips in a low-profile pill row. Below this header area, the interior splits into two columns: a vertically stacked filters sidebar on the left with well-spaced dropdowns and numeric controls, and a grid of program cards on the right that fills most of the horizontal space. Each program card is a tidy, medium-sized tile with balanced padding, arranged in a responsive grid that breathes. At the bottom right of the grid, pagination controls anchor the module, aligning with the sort dropdown above and reinforcing the rightward leg of the F-shaped reading flow. Empty and error states gracefully replace the grid area when needed, staying centered within the right column. Overall, the layout feels like a single, confident editorial panel for program discovery, with clear hierarchy, plenty of white space, and a subtle nod to the original hero-card + side-control composition in the way the left filters and right content are arranged.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The programs initializer is positioned logically at the very top of the main content column, directly beneath the fixed header stack and just above the large programs card module. It occupies no visible space but conceptually sits aligned to the left edge of the central content container, spanning its full width. Any visual components (filters bar, results summary, grid) appear below it, so it initializes the state for everything inside the main programs module.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual, zero-height component that loads and manages state before any visible programs-related content renders; it scrolls with the page but does not affect layout spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Inside the large programs card module, the filters panel forms a vertical sidebar along the left side of the internal content area. It starts a short distance (about 32px) below the applied-filters bar, and its top aligns horizontally with the first row of program cards on the right. On desktop, it occupies roughly 30–35% of the module’s internal width and stretches downward as needed, with 24–32px internal padding on all sides. The panel has a subtle card-like background or faint border to distinguish it from the main module while maintaining the same clean, corporate aesthetic. On smaller screens, this panel collapses to a hidden drawer that can slide in from the left or appear full-width above the programs list, but still visually reads as a distinct, well-padded block.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: On desktop and tablet, the filters panel behaves as a sticky sidebar within the main programs module: its top edge pins a short distance beneath the applied-filters bar and remains visible while scrolling through longer lists of programs, until the bottom of the module is reached. On mobile, it becomes a toggleable overlay or slide-out, appearing above the results when opened.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Inside the filters panel, the category filter is placed at the very top as the first control. It spans nearly the full width of the panel’s inner area, leaving consistent 24–32px side padding. Vertically, it sits about 8–12px below the panel’s title or section heading (if present), and there is a comfortable 16–24px gap below it before the next filter. Its dropdown field has enough height and whitespace to feel airy and easy to tap, matching the 8–12px vertical padding standard from the navigation and main controls.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the filters panel, scrolling with the panel’s content if the sidebar height exceeds the viewport; always remains the topmost filter control.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The duration filter sits directly beneath the category filter, aligned to the same left and right insets inside the filters panel. It uses the same full-width dropdown style, with a 16–24px vertical gap from the bottom of the category filter to maintain a clean stacked rhythm. It visually forms the second item in a straight vertical column of controls, contributing to the clear, conservative sidebar structure.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element within the filters panel; it scrolls within the sidebar as needed and maintains its sequential position directly below the category filter.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The price filter appears below the duration filter, maintaining the same left and right alignment inside the sidebar. As a numeric filter (range slider and/or min/max inputs), it occupies slightly more vertical space. There is a 16–24px gap above it for breathing room, and internally it has 8–12px spacing between label, controls, and any helper text. It sits visually as the third block in the column, with enough room so each price control is clearly distinguishable and not cramped.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the filters panel, extending vertically as needed; on smaller screens, it collapses neatly with the rest of the filters into the same stacked full-width arrangement.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The rating filter is placed beneath the price filter, continuing the single-column stack. A 16–24px vertical gap separates it from the price controls to avoid visual merging. It uses a full-width control (e.g., star selection or rating threshold dropdown) and aligns flush with the left and right padding of the filters panel. This positions rating as the fourth main grouping, completing the primary filter stack in a balanced, evenly spaced column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element at the lower portion of the main filter set; it scrolls inside the sidebar and maintains its position below the price filter across breakpoints.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The applied-filters bar runs horizontally across the full internal width of the main programs card, directly beneath the top control band that contains the results meta and sort control. It sits about 16–24px below that band, forming a second, slimmer strip of controls. The bar is low-profile in height but well-padded (8–12px vertical, 16–24px horizontal), hosting filter chips aligned to the left and wrapping to a second line if necessary. The right side of the bar remains relatively open or houses a subtle 'Clear all' action, reinforcing the spacious, editorial feel. Visually it acts as a delicate divider between the top summary/sort area and the main body containing the sidebar and grid.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: On larger screens, the applied-filters bar can behave as a subtle sticky element: as the user scrolls within the main module, it remains pinned just below the fixed header and above the filters/grid, ensuring users always see which filters are active. On smaller screens, it scrolls with the content but remains positioned directly above the visible programs list.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sort control is positioned in the upper right corner of the main programs card’s top band. This band spans the full width of the card, and the sort dropdown is right-aligned within it, occupying roughly the width of a standard button plus label. It sits on the same horizontal line as the results-meta (which is left-aligned), with around 24–32px padding above and below to create a comfortable, header-like strip. The sort control has 8–12px vertical padding within the field and 16–24px horizontal padding, mirroring the navigation link sizing and reinforcing the conservative, corporate feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The sort control remains anchored to the top-right of the main programs card. If the top band becomes sticky (along with the applied-filters bar), the sort control stays visible at the top of the viewport when scrolling through the results.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The results meta component occupies the upper left portion of the main programs card’s top band, directly opposite the sort control. It is left-aligned and spans about one-third to half of the band’s width, depending on text length, with the remaining space forming a comfortable gap between it and the right-aligned sort control. It sits with generous top and bottom padding (24–32px) to mimic a hero-card header, making the count and context (e.g., '32 programs found') easy to scan as the first focal point when entering the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Anchored at the top-left of the main programs card. If the header band is implemented as sticky, the results-meta stays visible at the top of the viewport as users scroll through the programs list.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The programs list occupies the right-hand portion of the main programs card’s interior, beginning just below the applied-filters bar and aligned horizontally with the top of the filters panel on the left. It spans roughly 65–70% of the card’s internal width and is inset from the card’s right edge by a consistent 24–32px padding, creating a tall, column-like area reminiscent of the dominant hero card in the mockup but filled with multiple tiles instead of a single image. Within this area, program cards are laid out in a responsive grid: on large screens, 3 cards per row; on medium screens, 2 per row; and on small screens, 1 per row. The vertical spacing between rows is around 24px, with similar horizontal gaps, ensuring the grid feels airy rather than crowded. The list extends downward, forming the main scrolling content of the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The programs list scrolls vertically within the page, bounded horizontally by the main card’s internal padding. It remains visually tied to the right of the filters sidebar on larger screens, and on small screens it shifts to full-width below the filters (which may be collapsed or toggled).

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each program card is a medium-sized, rectangular tile within the programs list grid. On desktop, a single card typically occupies about one-third of the programs-list width minus horizontal gaps, giving it a comfortable width that can show an image or color block at the top and text details below. The card has 24–32px internal padding on all sides, with a subtle shadow or border to echo the card-like hero style from the mockup. The top portion (such as a thumbnail or gradient band) anchors the card visually, while title and key meta are left-aligned beneath it with 8–16px vertical separation. Cards in a row are evenly spaced with consistent gaps and share equal height where possible, producing a clean, aligned grid that reads quickly as users scan horizontally then vertically down the column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Program cards are static within the grid, resizing responsively based on the number of columns: they expand to fill available width when the grid shifts to 2 or 1 column layouts on smaller screens, maintaining consistent internal padding and vertical rhythm.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pagination controls are placed directly beneath the programs list grid, aligned with the right edge of the programs-list column. There is a generous 32–48px gap between the last row of program cards and the pagination row to create a clear section break. The controls themselves sit on a single horizontal line, with page numbers and next/previous buttons arranged from left to right but anchored to the right side of the grid area. This alignment mirrors the sort control above, reinforcing the right edge as a secondary focal line in the vertical flow and echoing the mockup’s subtle right-side carousel hint.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The pagination controls scroll with the programs list and remain at the bottom of the main module content. As users navigate pages, the controls stay in the same location relative to the grid, providing a stable, predictable interaction point.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When no programs match the current filters, the empty-state replaces the programs list area within the right column of the main programs card. It is centered horizontally in that column and occupies a generous vertical block roughly 40–50% of the viewport height, creating a calm, uncluttered space. An icon or illustration sits near the top center of this block, followed by a brief message and optional action button, all stacked with 16–24px vertical spacing and plenty of white space around them. The filters sidebar on the left remains visible, emphasizing that the user can adjust filters to discover programs.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The empty-state appears in place of the programs grid and pagination controls, using the same right-column footprint. It scrolls with the page and remains visually anchored to the filters sidebar, disappearing once results are available again.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The error-state appears in the same general area as the programs list but with slightly higher prominence. It spans the full width of the main programs card’s interior (covering both where the filters sidebar and grid would normally inhabit, or at least stretching across the entire right column and overlapping the column boundary visually) and sits immediately below the top control band (results-meta and sort). It is presented as a distinct banner-like card with stronger contrast (e.g., a soft red-tinted background) and 24–32px padding, containing an error icon on the left and explanatory text and actions to the right. There is ample whitespace above and below this block to avoid visual noise.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: When active, the error-state temporarily replaces the programs list, empty-state, and pagination controls. It pushes the filters sidebar down if it spans full width, or appears centered in the main content area if a narrower layout is preferred. It scrolls with the content but typically appears high in the viewport so that users notice it immediately.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart link lives in the main site header, sharing the horizontal navigation row. The logo is positioned at the far left of the header, the primary navigation links are centered, and the cart link is aligned to the far right end of that same row, maintaining balance within the full-width header band. It has similar padding to the nav links (8–12px vertical, 16–24px horizontal) but may be slightly emphasized through an icon or subtle accent color. The header band has generous top and bottom padding so the cart link feels comfortably spaced within a clean, corporate navigation strip.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The cart link is part of a fixed (sticky) header that remains pinned to the top of the viewport as users scroll. It maintains its position at the right edge of the navigation row across breakpoints, collapsing into a right-aligned icon within a mobile menu when screen width is limited.

name: Program Detail
title: Program Detail
description: Displays detailed information for a single online program and allows the user to select a tier and start date, then add the enrollment to the cart.

core_features: Read the program_id from the URL and fetch program details (including available tiers and start date constraints) via getProgramDetail.
Display key program information such as name, category, duration in weeks, delivery format, description, rating, and tags.
Present all available ProgramTier options for the program, highlighting the default tier and allowing the user to change their selection.
Display start date constraints (earliest and latest allowed dates) and enforce them in a start date selector.
Validate user selections (tier and start date) before enrollment.
Add the selected program tier and start date to the cart via addProgramEnrollmentToCart, handling success and error states.
Provide navigation back to the program list (programs.html) with the program category as a URL parameter.
Provide navigation to the cart page (cart.html) after a successful add-to-cart action.
user_workflows:
View program details:
1. User navigates to program_detail.html with a program_id URL parameter.
2. On page initialization, the page reads program_id from the URL query string.
3. The page calls getProgramDetail with the programId derived from program_id.
4. While data is loading, a loading state is displayed instead of program content.
5. On success, the page displays program information (name, category, description, duration_weeks, delivery_format, rating, rating_count, tags) and the list of tiers.
6. If getProgramDetail returns an error or no program, an error state is displayed with an option to navigate back to programs.html.

Select tier and start date, then add to cart:
1. After program details load, the tier selector component checks the tiers array.
2. If one or more tiers are available, the component pre-selects the tier where is_default is true, or the first tier if no default flag is set.
3. The start date selector initializes with earliest_start_date and latest_start_date from start_date_constraints and, if present in the URL, a preselected start_date.
4. The user optionally changes the tier using a dropdown or radio list; the selected tier is stored in page state and optionally reflected in the URL as a tier_id parameter.
5. The user selects a start date within the allowed range using a date picker; the chosen value is stored in state and optionally reflected in the URL as a start_date parameter.
6. The user clicks the Add to Cart / Enroll button.
7. The page validates that a tier is selected and that the selected start date is within earliest_start_date and latest_start_date.
8. If validation passes, the page calls addProgramEnrollmentToCart with programId (from program.id), programTierId (from selected tier.tier_id), and start_date (the selected date in ISO format).
9. On success, the page shows a success confirmation message and enables or highlights a View Cart button that navigates to cart.html.
10. On failure, the page shows the returned message and allows the user to retry.

Navigate back to all programs:
1. From the program detail view, the user clicks a Back to all programs link or a breadcrumb element.
2. The page reads program.category from the loaded program.
3. The browser navigates to programs.html with a category URL parameter set to the program.category value.
4. The programs.html page uses this parameter to pre-filter or highlight programs in that category.

Handle missing or invalid program_id:
1. User opens program_detail.html without a program_id URL parameter or with an invalid value.
2. On initialization, the page attempts to read program_id; if it is missing or empty, the page shows an error state indicating that no program has been selected and provides a link to programs.html.
3. If program_id is present but getProgramDetail fails (e.g., program not found), the error handler displays an appropriate message and a link back to programs.html.
interactions:
Page load / initialization:
- Trigger: The browser loads program_detail.html.
- Action: A controller component parses the program_id, optional tier_id, and optional start_date from the URL query string.
- Action: If program_id is valid, it calls getProgramDetail with programId set to program_id.
- Action: Sets an internal loading state until the response is received or an error occurs.

Tier selection:
- Trigger: User changes the selected tier using a dropdown, radio list, or similar control.
- Action: The selected tier_id is stored in page state.
- Action: Optionally, the URL query string is updated (e.g., adding or updating a tier_id parameter) without reloading the page to preserve selection for sharing or refresh.
- Action: The enrollment panel updates displayed price and tier description based on the selected tier.

Start date selection:
- Trigger: User picks a start date using a date input that respects earliest_start_date and latest_start_date.
- Action: The chosen date is validated client-side to ensure it lies within the constraints.
- Action: The selected date is stored in page state.
- Action: Optionally, the URL query string is updated (e.g., adding or updating a start_date parameter) without reloading the page to preserve selection for refresh or sharing.

Add to Cart / Enroll click:
- Trigger: User clicks the Add to Cart or Enroll button.
- Action: Client-side validation runs, ensuring a tier is selected and a valid start date is chosen.
- Action: If validation fails, inline error messages are displayed near the relevant fields and no API call is made.
- Action: If validation passes, the button enters a submitting/disabled state and the page calls addProgramEnrollmentToCart with the current programId, selected programTierId, and selected start_date.
- Action: On a successful response (success === true), the page displays a success state/message, possibly including data from message, and stores the returned cart_item_id in local component state if needed for display.
- Action: On failure (success === false) or error, the page displays the message from the API and re-enables the button for retry.

View Cart navigation:
- Trigger: User clicks a View Cart button or link presented after a successful add-to-cart (or in a persistent header/cart icon if present globally).
- Action: The browser navigates to cart.html without additional parameters (or with any globally required parameters, if defined elsewhere).

Back to all programs navigation:
- Trigger: User clicks a Back to all programs link or a breadcrumb element.
- Action: The page uses program.category from the loaded program data.
- Action: The browser navigates to programs.html with a category query parameter set to the program.category value.

Error and retry interactions:
- Trigger: getProgramDetail returns an error or fails.
- Action: An error message is shown with a Retry button and/or a Back to all programs link.
- Trigger: User clicks Retry.
- Action: The page reissues the getProgramDetail call with the same programId and re-enters a loading state.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The page relies on URL parameters and API responses to manage its state. The primary required parameter is program_id in the query string (e.g., program_detail.html?program_id={PROGRAM_ID}). On initialization, the page controller reads program_id; if it is missing or invalid, the page enters an 'invalid program' error state and suggests returning to programs.html.

After reading program_id, the controller calls getProgramDetail with programId set to the program_id value. While waiting for the response, a 'loading' state is active; no program details or enrollment controls are shown. When the API returns successfully, the page stores the program, tiers, and start_date_constraints from the response in in-memory state. A 'loaded' state is then active, enabling all components that depend on this data.

Initial tier selection is determined from the tiers array: the page looks for a tier where is_default is true. If found, its tier_id becomes the selected tier_id in state; otherwise, the first tier in the list may be used as the fallback. If an optional URL parameter tier_id is present and matches one of the available tiers, it overrides the default logic and becomes the initial selection.

For the start date, start_date_constraints.earliest_start_date and start_date_constraints.latest_start_date determine the valid range. The component may also read an optional start_date parameter from the URL. If present and within the allowed range, this value becomes the initial selected start date; if not, no date is preselected and the user must choose one. The start date selector enforces the min and max constraints so that the user cannot pick an invalid date.

Whenever the user changes the selected tier or start date, the in-memory state is updated. Optionally, the page updates the URL query string using history APIs (without reloading) to keep tier_id and start_date in sync with the user's current selection. This makes the page shareable and resilient to reloads while still relying solely on URL parameters to persist state.

When the user clicks Add to Cart / Enroll, the page uses the current in-memory state (program.id, selectedTierId, selectedStartDate) and sends them to addProgramEnrollmentToCart. The result does not change the URL but updates local state to reflect success or failure. On success, the page enters a 'post-add' state where a success message and View Cart navigation are available. On failure, the state includes error details and keeps the enrollment controls active for retry.

layout.grid_system: Visually consistent with a 12-column responsive grid centered in the viewport, with a max-width content container and substantial left/right margins.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows a linear, top-to-bottom flow anchored by a fixed-height utility bar and a full-width header, then a dominant hero-like program card in the main content. The Program Detail content is organized into a tall, visually strong primary panel on the left (program hero and key info) and a narrower, right-side vertical band that hints at additional programs or related content, echoing the carousel/slider cue from the mockup. Below the hero region, secondary information such as detailed description, modules, and FAQs continue in a single, stacked column within the same centered content container.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows a linear, top-to-bottom flow anchored by a fixed-height utility bar and a full-width header, then a dominant hero-like program card in the main content. The Program Detail content is organized into a tall, visually strong primary panel on the left (program hero and key info) and a narrower, right-side vertical band that hints at additional programs or related content, echoing the carousel/slider cue from the mockup. Below the hero region, secondary information such as detailed description, modules, and FAQs continue in a single, stacked column within the same centered content container.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The utility bar is shallow and spans 100% of the viewport width, with minimal vertical height (around 40px). The main header below it occupies moderate height (around 80–96px) with generous vertical padding around the logo and navigation. The hero program card below fills roughly 60–70% of the viewport height on initial load, occupying about two-thirds to three-quarters of the centered 12-column content width on the left, while the remaining right side is reserved for a slim vertical band containing a vertical divider and compact controls or thumbnails. Subsequent sections (program details, curriculum, testimonials) maintain full-width within the content container but feel visually secondary due to reduced height and simpler backgrounds.
layout_strategies.content_density: Overall density remains low and editorial: large blocks of whitespace separate the major sections, especially around the hero program card and section dividers. Text is grouped into short, scannable clusters: key program meta and call-to-action are tightly grouped in the hero card, while long-form description is broken into subsections with clear headings, generous line spacing, and comfortable margins. On the hero, minimal copy is used, focusing on title, tier, duration, and core CTA, with supporting information visually de-emphasized. This keeps the page calm and premium despite the relatively content-heavy nature of a program detail page.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The user’s eye is led from the top-left logo through the centered navigation, then down into the bold, blue-gradient hero program card. Within the hero, the eye first catches the program mark or abstract graphic, then the program title and essential meta details, followed by the prominent enrollment controls (tier selection, start date, primary CTA). The right-side vertical band and its partially visible card or arrow suggest additional horizontally scrollable content (other programs or success stories), subtly pulling attention to the right edge. Scrolling down, section headings with clear typographic hierarchy and comfortable spacing guide the eye in an F-shaped reading pattern through description, what’s included, and any supporting content like testimonials or FAQs.

layout.overall_description:
The Program Detail page presents as a clean, centered layout on a neutral light background, framed within a max-width 12-column grid. At the very top stretches a thin utility bar across the full width of the viewport, sitting flush against the browser’s top edge. Directly beneath, a full-width header band houses the logo on the far left and a horizontally centered navigation cluster. The header feels substantial but airy, with generous top and bottom padding so that the logo and nav links sit comfortably in the vertical center of the band.

Below the header, the main content container is centered, with wide left and right margins relative to the viewport. The upper fold is dominated by a large program hero card: a tall, visually impactful rectangle with a bold blue-green gradient background occupying around two-thirds to three-quarters of the container’s width and roughly the upper 60–70% of the visible vertical space. Inside this card, a simple abstract program mark or brand motif is positioned near the top-left, with the program title and a concise subtitle immediately beneath or to the right, set in white or near-white text for strong contrast. Essential meta information such as duration, intensity level, and coaching format is aligned in a tidy cluster below the title, followed by a compact enrollment area where tier selection controls, a start-date selector, and a primary “Add to cart / Enroll” button are grouped in a horizontal or vertically stacked arrangement near the lower-left to mid-left of the card. The bottom and top edges of this hero block are surrounded by generous whitespace, reinforcing its importance.

To the immediate right of this hero card, within the same horizontal band and sharing its vertical alignment, a narrow vertical column extends from roughly the top to the bottom of the hero area. This column begins with a thin vertical dividing line that visually separates it from the main hero, leaving a slim gutter. Within this right column, a partially visible rectangular tile or card peeks in from the far right edge, suggesting a horizontally scrollable carousel of other programs or related success stories. Small arrow controls or pagination indicators may be centered vertically within this band, reinforcing the idea of horizontal movement without crowding the hero.

As the user scrolls past this hero region, the layout transitions into a stacked, single-column arrangement within the same centered container. First, a succinct summary or “Program overview” block appears, spanning the full content width with ample top margin from the hero and clear left alignment. Below this, a series of full-width sections follow: detailed curriculum or weekly breakdown, what’s included, coach bio, testimonials, and FAQs. Each section is separated by generous vertical spacing, with headings anchored to the left, body text set in a comfortable line length, and occasional supporting visuals (icons or small images) aligned to the left or centered in the column. The visual hierarchy keeps the hero and enrollment focus at the top while allowing users to scroll down for depth, maintaining the same calm, professional atmosphere and the grid-driven order observed in the original mockup.

name: Classes Schedule
title: Classes Schedule
description: Displays an interactive, filterable schedule of group classes for a selected date, allowing users to browse sessions, avoid time conflicts, and navigate to detailed booking.

core_features: On load, read URL parameters (date, time_of_day, intensity, max_duration), initialize page state, and fetch filter metadata via getClassScheduleFilterOptions.
Use getClassSchedule with the selected date and filters to retrieve the list of GroupClassSession entries to display.
Provide a date selection control that updates the date parameter and re-requests the schedule for the chosen day.
Provide time-of-day, intensity, and maximum-duration filters, with options populated exclusively from getClassScheduleFilterOptions.
Display each class session with start time, end time, duration, intensity, focus, remaining spots, and capacity, using dynamic data from getClassSchedule.
Order sessions by start time and detect overlaps between sessions so that users can identify non-overlapping class combinations.
Allow clicking on a specific session to navigate to class_detail.html with the corresponding session_id parameter.
Provide navigation to cart.html (e.g., via a cart icon or link) without altering the schedule state.
Keep the current filters and date in the URL query so that reloading or sharing the URL restores the same schedule view.
user_workflows: Browse a specific date’s schedule: (1) User navigates to classes.html with or without URL parameters. (2) Page reads date, time_of_day, intensity, and max_duration from the URL. (3) Page calls getClassScheduleFilterOptions to populate available filter options. (4) Page calls getClassSchedule with the current date and filters. (5) Sessions are displayed in time order, with overlaps highlighted. (6) User scans the list to see available classes and remaining spots.

Change the schedule date: (1) User opens the date picker in the filter bar. (2) User selects a different calendar date. (3) Component updates the date query parameter in the URL (replacing any existing date) without altering other filters. (4) Page calls getClassSchedule with the new date and existing filters. (5) Schedule list re-renders with sessions for the new date.

Filter by time-of-day, intensity, and duration: (1) User opens a time-of-day, intensity, or max-duration control whose options come from getClassScheduleFilterOptions. (2) User selects or clears an option. (3) Component updates the corresponding URL parameters (time_of_day, intensity, max_duration). (4) Page triggers getClassSchedule with the updated filters and current date. (5) Schedule list updates to show only matching sessions.

Identify non-overlapping classes: (1) After sessions are loaded via getClassSchedule, the page sorts them by start time. (2) An overlap detection function compares each session’s start and end to others. (3) Non-overlapping sessions can be visually grouped or labelled; overlapping sessions are indicated as conflicting. (4) User uses these indicators to choose combinations of sessions that do not overlap.

Open class details and booking: (1) User clicks on a specific class session in the schedule. (2) Component reads that session’s group_class_session_id from the getClassSchedule response. (3) Browser navigates to class_detail.html with URL parameter session_id set to the clicked group_class_session_id. (4) Any required return parameters (e.g., the originating date) can be handled by class_detail.html if needed, but navigation from this page only sends the session_id as specified.

Go to cart: (1) User clicks a cart icon or a 'View Cart' link visible on the classes schedule page. (2) Browser navigates directly to cart.html without modifying schedule URL parameters or making additional API calls on this page.
interactions: Date change interaction: onDateChange from the date picker updates the date query parameter, triggers a new getClassSchedule call, and refreshes the results.
Time-of-day filter interaction: onTimeOfDayChange updates the time_of_day query parameter, then calls getClassSchedule with the updated filters while preserving the current date.
Intensity filter interaction: onIntensityChange updates or removes the intensity query parameter and triggers a new getClassSchedule call.
Max duration filter interaction: onMaxDurationChange updates or clears the max_duration query parameter, then refreshes the schedule via getClassSchedule.
Session click interaction: onSessionClick takes the selected session’s group_class_session_id and navigates to class_detail.html?session_id=<value>.
Cart navigation interaction: onCartClick navigates to cart.html without changing the schedule URL or making additional API requests.

Manual refresh interaction (if implemented): onRefreshClick re-invokes getClassSchedule using the current date and filters from URL parameters, to ensure the schedule reflects the latest availability.
Empty-state handling: if getClassSchedule returns no sessions, onScheduleLoaded triggers display of an empty-state message instead of session rows.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page uses URL query parameters as the single source of truth for state. On initial load, the page reads date, time_of_day, intensity, and max_duration from the URL. If date is missing or invalid, a runtime default (such as the current date) is chosen without being hardcoded in the design; this default is then reflected into the URL so that state remains shareable. The page first calls getClassScheduleFilterOptions to obtain valid filter options and render the filter controls. Next, it constructs a filters object for getClassSchedule based on current URL parameters: time_of_day is mapped from the URL string to the matching key in the time_of_day_ranges returned by getClassScheduleFilterOptions; intensity is used only if it matches one of the returned intensities values; max_duration is parsed from the URL and used only if it matches one of the available max_duration_options. Any invalid or unsupported parameter values are ignored when building the filters object and are optionally removed from the URL. Whenever the user changes date or filters, the corresponding query parameters are updated in the URL (using navigation or history APIs) before calling getClassSchedule again. This ensures that reloading the page or sharing the URL reproduces the same date and filter configuration. No localStorage or other client-side persistence mechanisms are used; all state is derived from the current URL and live API responses.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial data loading, URL parameter parsing, interface calls, and communication between filter controls and the schedule display.
Provides date selection and filter controls (time-of-day, intensity, maximum duration) with options sourced from getClassScheduleFilterOptions, and updates URL parameters when user selections change.

Displays the list of class sessions returned by getClassSchedule, sorted by start time, and uses overlap detection to mark sessions that conflict in time so users can identify non-overlapping options.

Represents a single session row or tile within the schedule, showing class_name, focus, intensity, start and end times, duration_minutes, remaining_spots, and capacity; handles click to navigate to class_detail.html using the session’s group_class_session_id.

Receives the array of sessions from getClassSchedule, compares start_datetime and end_datetime fields to determine which sessions overlap in time, and annotates or flags each session with overlap information for the classes-schedule-list component.
Shows a context-aware message when getClassSchedule returns an empty sessions list for the current date and filters, and may prompt the user to adjust filters or pick another date.
Provides user access to the cart page from the classes schedule (e.g., via a cart icon or 'View Cart' text link) by navigating to cart.html without changing current schedule state.

layout.grid_system: Visually consistent with a 12-column responsive grid centered in the viewport, with a max-width content container and substantial left/right margins.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the mockup’s linear top-to-bottom structure: a full-width, shallow utility bar at the very top (housing the cart link), then a full-width header band with logo on the left and centered global navigation. Below this fixed header block, the Classes Schedule content sits in a centered max-width container. Within this main container, a dominant, card-like schedule panel (the page controller + filters + list) occupies roughly the left two-thirds to three-quarters of the width. A narrow vertical band on the right (within the same container) remains mostly white with a subtle vertical divider, used for secondary information or contextual hints, echoing the partner/hero + carousel-control relationship from the mockup.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the mockup’s linear top-to-bottom structure: a full-width, shallow utility bar at the very top (housing the cart link), then a full-width header band with logo on the left and centered global navigation. Below this fixed header block, the Classes Schedule content sits in a centered max-width container. Within this main container, a dominant, card-like schedule panel (the page controller + filters + list) occupies roughly the left two-thirds to three-quarters of the width. A narrow vertical band on the right (within the same container) remains mostly white with a subtle vertical divider, used for secondary information or contextual hints, echoing the partner/hero + carousel-control relationship from the mockup.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The utility bar is shallow (~32px–40px tall) and spans the full viewport width. The header below it uses generous vertical padding (~24px–32px top and bottom). The main schedule panel fills most of the remaining above-the-fold area, roughly 60–70% of the viewport height, with ample white space above and below. Horizontally, the main schedule card uses about 8–9 of 12 columns (~66–75% of the max-width content container), leaving about 3–4 columns (~25–34%) on the right as a light, open band separated by a thin vertical rule. Inside the card, the top band (title and key date context) and filter bar occupy the upper ~25–30% of the card height; the schedule list or empty state fills the rest.
layout_strategies.content_density: The layout keeps a low, editorial density: large blocks of whitespace around the main schedule card, minimal copy, and clearly separated structural elements. The filter bar uses concise labels and a limited number of controls in a single horizontal row where possible. Each class session item has generous vertical padding and spacing between rows (~16–24px), ensuring that time and class details are easy to scan and not cramped. The right-hand vertical band remains mostly empty aside from subtle secondary elements, reinforcing the spacious, professional tone.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow mirrors the F-shaped pattern from the mockup: the eye lands first on the top-left logo in the header, then sweeps right across the centered navigation and the cart link in the utility/header region. From there, the bold blue gradient or strong visual accent at the top of the main schedule card draws attention downward into the Classes Schedule content. Within the card, the title/date and filter controls at the top are scanned from left to right, then the eye moves downward through the vertical list of class sessions. Finally, the eye is gently pulled toward the right by the subtle vertical divider and the mostly empty right band, which may include small contextual hints, reinforcing the sense that additional navigational or contextual content could exist there without competing with the main schedule.

layout.overall_description: The page presents a polished, corporate layout with a clear hierarchy. A slim utility bar spans the full width across the very top, followed by a fixed header band with the logo on the left and main navigation centered, echoing the mockup’s composed, balanced top region. Beneath this, the main content is centered within a max-width (12-column) container, leaving generous margins on both sides. Dominating the visible viewport is a large, card-like Classes Schedule panel aligned to the left side of this container, about two-thirds to three-quarters of the container’s width. Its upper portion carries a strong blue gradient or branded accent behind the page title and high-level date context, visually reminiscent of the hero card from the mockup. Immediately below, a horizontal strip of filters spans the card’s width, followed by a vertically stacked schedule list that fills the remainder of the card. To the right of this main card, a narrow vertical band of whitespace runs from near the top of the schedule card downwards, separated by a thin, subtle vertical rule. This right band stays visually calm and lightly populated, allowing the bold schedule card to remain the hero, while hinting at potential controls or contextual content. The net effect is a modern yet conservative layout with substantial breathing room, clear focus on the schedule, and a gentle left-to-right, top-to-bottom flow that feels intuitive and professional for a sports consultancy and wellbeing services brand.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The classes-page-controller is the main visual ‘hero’ panel of the page and sits in the primary content column beneath the fixed header. It is centered within the max-width content container but aligned to the left grid segment, occupying roughly 8–9 of 12 grid columns (~66–75% of the container width). The top of this card starts about 64px below the bottom edge of the header band, following the generous vertical spacing pattern. The card itself has substantial internal padding on all sides (~32px) and a slightly elevated feel (e.g., subtle shadow or border) to echo the mockup’s hero tile. The upper third of the card is reserved for the page-level schedule controls: a bold page title like “Classes Schedule” aligned left, a smaller subheading or selected date range directly below or to the right of it, and potentially a prominent highlighted strip behind them using the brand’s blue gradient. This gradient strip spans the full width of the card’s content area and is about 96–128px tall, visually mirroring the hero visual in the mockup while framing the schedule content. Below this title/date region, the schedule-filter-bar runs full width inside the card, and below that the classes-schedule-list or schedule-empty-state occupies the remainder of the card’s vertical space. The right edge of this card aligns with a subtle vertical divider that separates it from the right-side white band, reinforcing the sense that the card is the dominant content block in an otherwise spacious layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the normal document flow but designed to occupy roughly 60–70% of the viewport height on initial load. On wider screens, it maintains its width in columns while staying centered; on smaller screens, it expands to full width with margins reduced, preserving internal padding.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The schedule-filter-bar sits immediately below the page title/date region inside the classes-page-controller card, forming a distinct horizontal band across the full internal width of the card. It is separated from the title area above by roughly 16–24px of vertical whitespace and from the schedule list below by another 16–24px, aligning with the 8–24px spacing scale. The bar itself is about 64–88px tall, depending on the number of controls, and has internal horizontal padding that matches the card’s padding (so its left and right edges align perfectly with the card content edge). Filter controls (e.g., date selector, time-of-day toggles, class type dropdown, location filter) are laid out in a single row from left to right on desktop, with generous horizontal gaps (~16px–24px) between them, keeping the overall look clean and uncluttered. The primary filter (e.g., the date selector) is aligned towards the left, visually anchoring the bar and forming a strong vertical column with the page title above and the schedule list below. On smaller screens, the bar can wrap into two rows but continues to feel like one cohesive band. Visually, the filter bar uses a lighter background than the gradient title strip (often plain white or very light grey) with a subtle bottom border, reinforcing its role as a functional control panel distinct from the content below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Within the card, the filter bar can use a sticky behavior: when the user scrolls the schedule list, the filter bar remains pinned to the top edge of the viewport or the top edge of the card area, ensuring filters are always accessible while the schedule content scrolls beneath it.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The classes-schedule-list occupies the main body of the classes-page-controller card beneath the schedule-filter-bar. It spans the full internal width of the card and fills all remaining vertical space in the card’s visible area, extending below the fold if there are many sessions. The top of the list starts approximately 16–24px below the filter bar, providing a clear visual separation. Each class-session-item inside the list is arranged in a vertical stack, with consistent spacing between items (~16–24px) to maintain a low-density, easy-to-scan rhythm. The list alignment is left-anchored, with the start times lined up along a strong vertical axis near the card’s left edge, creating a visual timeline effect. Optional lightweight horizontal rules or subtle card boundaries help separate sessions without making the list feel heavy. If the schedule is long, the overall page scrolls, with the list smoothly continuing downward while the right-side white band remains visually present at the top of the viewport, similar to the mockup’s hero card and carousel control relationship. On smaller screens, the list becomes a full-width column below the filter bar, preserving the same vertical spacing and rhythm.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls vertically within the page as part of the main document flow. When the filter bar is sticky, the list scrolls behind/under the filters, maintaining the filters at the top of the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each class-session-item is rendered as a horizontal card-like row within the classes-schedule-list, spanning nearly the full width of the list area (minus the card’s internal padding). The height of each item is generous, typically 64–96px, depending on content such as the class name, instructor, and location. Each item has internal padding on all sides (~16–24px) and a subtle visual boundary: either a light border, a soft shadow, or a slightly different background shade, echoing the refined card language of the mockup without overwhelming the page. Content inside the item follows a left-to-right hierarchy: on the far left, the start time (and optionally end time) is aligned in a narrow column, forming a consistent vertical time rail across all items. Immediately to the right of the time, the class title and primary details stack vertically, and on the far right of the row, secondary meta information such as duration or capacity may be right-aligned. Vertical spacing between items is consistent (~16–24px), so each card feels distinct with air around it. If two classes overlap in time, the vertical positioning and any visual treatment directed by the schedule-overlap-helper ensure they are still visually readable, potentially with a slight offset or label indicating conflict while preserving the clean, linear list appearance.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-sticky and part of the natural vertical stack within the list. The item layout gracefully adapts on smaller screens, with the time and meta information stacking above or below the title while preserving clear alignment and spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The schedule-overlap-helper is not directly visible as a standalone UI element but subtly influences the spatial arrangement of class-session-item components within the classes-schedule-list. Conceptually, it reserves and manages a narrow vertical column along the left side of the list (the time rail area) and uses that logic to prevent overlapping sessions from visually colliding. When two or more class-session-items overlap in time, this helper ensures each item receives sufficient vertical offset and spacing so that their time labels remain clearly readable and the linear sequence of items still respects the overall vertical rhythm of the layout. It may also introduce small indicators adjacent to affected items (such as a slim colored bar or an icon near the time) while preserving the overall clean and low-density aesthetic. The result is that, even with complex overlaps, the list still appears ordered, evenly spaced, and easy to scan, with no items visually stacked or squeezed unnaturally.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Logical and structural rather than visual; it dynamically influences the vertical positions and spacing of class-session-items within the list but does not occupy its own explicit block in the layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The schedule-empty-state occupies the same spatial region as the classes-schedule-list inside the classes-page-controller card whenever there are no available classes for the selected filters. Positioned directly below the schedule-filter-bar and aligned to the card’s internal padding, it is centered horizontally and vertically within the remaining card space to create a calm, balanced message. The empty state content (icon, headline, and supporting text) fits within a column roughly 50–60% of the card’s internal width, ensuring generous margins on both sides. Above and below the message, there is generous whitespace (~32–48px) to keep the layout feeling light and to emphasize the state. If a call-to-action (e.g., “Clear filters” or “View other days”) is included, it appears below the supporting text, centered and with ample padding around it, mirroring the generous button styling from the rest of the site. The blue gradient or brand accent from the title area remains visible above, anchoring the empty state in the same visual context as the normal schedule.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Shown conditionally in place of the classes-schedule-list. It remains static within the card and does not scroll independently; the overall page scroll behaves normally.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart-access-link lives in the top utility/header region, visually aligned with the mockup’s full-width utility bar concept. It is positioned at the far right of the shallow utility bar that spans the full viewport width above or integrated into the top-right corner of the main header band, depending on final header implementation. The link is horizontally aligned with any other small utility links but given slightly more emphasis through a stronger weight or small icon (e.g., a cart or bag) without becoming as dominant as the main navigation. The vertical padding around the link is modest (~8–12px), matching the utility bar’s height and maintaining the clean, conservative top region. Because it sits in the highest region of the layout, it becomes part of the initial F-pattern eye sweep: logo left, navigation centered, cart on the top-right. The link maintains at least 16–24px of space from the right edge of the viewport to avoid feeling cramped and aligns with the right boundary of the main content container below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Remains fixed to the top of the viewport as part of the sticky utility/header region. As the user scrolls through the schedule content, the cart-access-link stays visible at the top-right, providing constant access to bookings without interfering with the main schedule card.

name: Class Detail
title: Class Session Detail
description: Page for viewing details of a specific group class session and booking it for one or more attendees.

core_features: Load detailed information for a specific group class session using the session_id URL parameter and the getClassSessionDetail interface.
Display core session information: class name, focus, intensity, description, start time, end time, duration, remaining spots, capacity, location, and price.
Display trainer information associated with the class session (name, bio, rating) from the getClassSessionDetail response.
Provide booking controls that allow users to set the number of attendees (up to remaining spots) and enter contact details (name, email, phone).
