When the active category is nutrition coaching, displays the current list of nutrition bundles returned by searchNutritionBundles, including pagination controls; re-renders when filters, sort, or pagination state changes.

Represents an individual nutrition bundle result; displays dynamic attributes such as name, product_type, num_sessions, minutes_per_session, total_minutes, and price; clicking the card navigates to the bundle detail page.

Provides context-specific links from the selected coaching category to the consultations page; constructs a URL with `service_type` equal to the active category key when the user opts to explore 1:1 consultations.
Offers a persistent way to navigate to the cart page (cart.html) from within the Coaching & Services page, without modifying any URL parameters used for this page's internal state.

layout.grid_system: Visually consistent with a 12-column responsive grid centered in the viewport, with a max-width content container and substantial left/right margins.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows a stacked, top-to-bottom flow within a centered max-width container under the fixed global header. At the top of the Coaching & Services content area sits a slim, full-width consultation navigation block. Directly beneath it, a dominant hero-like category summary panel occupies the left two-thirds to three-quarters of the content area, with a reserved vertical utility rail on the right. Below the hero, the flow continues in a single main column: first the category selector, then the nutrition bundle filters, and then the bundle results list. The right-side vertical rail remains visually lighter and is used for persistent controls like cart access, echoing the carousel/control column from the mockup.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows a stacked, top-to-bottom flow within a centered max-width container under the fixed global header. At the top of the Coaching & Services content area sits a slim, full-width consultation navigation block. Directly beneath it, a dominant hero-like category summary panel occupies the left two-thirds to three-quarters of the content area, with a reserved vertical utility rail on the right. Below the hero, the flow continues in a single main column: first the category selector, then the nutrition bundle filters, and then the bundle results list. The right-side vertical rail remains visually lighter and is used for persistent controls like cart access, echoing the carousel/control column from the mockup.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The main content is centered in the viewport using a 12-column grid with generous left and right margins. The hero category summary panel occupies roughly 8–9 of the 12 columns (about 70–75% of the container width), while a narrow 3–4 column vertical band on the right (about 25–30%) is reserved for cart and utility controls. Vertical spacing between major sections (consultation navigation, hero, category selector, filters, results) is generous—around 48–64px—to maintain the open, editorial feel. Within components, padding follows the 24–32px range for cards and 8–16px for controls.
layout_strategies.content_density: The density is intentionally low and calm. Above-the-fold, the dominant visual is the large, colorful hero card with minimal, high-impact text and a prominent logo or category marker. The navigation block and category selector use succinct labels with ample spacing. Filters are presented in a single, readable horizontal band rather than a crowded panel. The results list uses clearly separated cards with enough whitespace between items (24–32px) to avoid a catalog-like clutter and to support a consultancy/wellbeing tone rather than an e-commerce feel.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow mimics the mockup’s F-shaped pattern. The user’s eye first hits the global logo and main nav (outside this scope), then drops into the page-level consultation navigation block near the top center. From there, the bold blue-green category summary panel pulls attention downward and slightly left. Within this hero, the category mark/logo is noticed first, then the concise headline and supporting text. The eye is then guided to the right by a subtle vertical divider to the slim utility rail where the cart access control sits, signaling ongoing actions like reviewing selections. Scrolling further, the eye moves back to the left main column to interact with the category selector and filters, then down through the vertical list/grid of bundle cards, while the right vertical rail remains a quiet but persistent presence.

layout.overall_description: Below the fixed global header and utility bar, the Coaching & Services page opens into a centered content area using a 12-column grid. A slim, full-width consultation navigation block sits near the top of this area, giving quick access to different consultation modes while leaving plenty of white space above and below. Dominating the upper fold on the left is a large, blue-gradient category summary panel that fills roughly two-thirds to three-quarters of the available width and about 60–70% of the viewport height, acting as the visual anchor for the page. To its right runs a narrow vertical strip, separated by a faint divider line; within this strip a clean, rectangular cart access control is positioned around the vertical center and remains visible as the user scrolls, echoing the mockup’s carousel control band. Beneath the hero, the layout transitions smoothly into a stacked, single-column flow in the main left area: first a category selector band, then a concise nutrition filter bar, and finally a spacious list or grid of nutrition bundle cards. Throughout, generous margins, clear alignment to the grid, and restrained use of color and typography create a professional, corporate look with a modern, editorial calm—supporting focused browsing of coaching services without visual overload.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page root defines the main content area for the Coaching & Services page beneath the fixed global utility bar and header. It spans the full browser width but constrains all inner content to a centered max-width container (approximately 1100–1280px) aligned to a 12-column grid. There is sufficient top padding (roughly 80–96px) to visually clear the fixed header. Inside this root, content flows vertically in a linear, stacked sequence: first the consultation-navigation-block near the top center, then the large category-summary-panel aligned to the left grid columns with the right-side vertical Rail (for cart-access-control), followed by the category-selector, the nutrition-bundle-filters, and finally the nutrition-bundle-results. Left and right margins outside the max-width container remain clean and empty, reinforcing the spacious, professional aesthetic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static container that governs overall max-width, centering, and vertical flow of all page components; it scrolls with the page and does not remain fixed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The category selector sits directly below the hero-like category-summary-panel within the main left content column. It is aligned to the same left grid edge as the hero, spanning roughly 8–9 of the 12 grid columns (about 70–75% of the container width) and leaving the right 3–4 columns for the vertical utility rail. Vertically, it is separated from the bottom edge of the hero by generous whitespace, roughly 48px, to clearly distinguish it as a new functional zone. Visually, the selector is organized as a horizontal row of category chips or tabs (e.g., ‘All’, ‘Nutrition’, ‘Performance’, ‘Wellbeing’), left aligned and wrapped to multiple lines if necessary. Each category item uses comfortable padding (8–12px vertical, 16–20px horizontal) and ample spacing between items (16–24px) to keep density low. On very wide screens, this band visually hugs the left side, giving the impression of a soft “side rail” that aligns with the mockup’s linear hierarchy while still maintaining the overall single-column feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls with the page; may become sticky just below the fixed header on larger screens once the hero scrolls out of view, keeping category switching accessible while maintaining its position along the main left column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The category summary panel is the dominant hero element in the main content area. It is positioned near the top of the coaching-services-page-root, appearing immediately below a modest vertical gap (around 32–48px) beneath the consultation-navigation-block. Horizontally, it is anchored to the left and spans about 8–9 grid columns (approximately 70–75% of the container width), leaving a deliberate vertical gap to its right. It sits flush against a subtle vertical divider line that marks the beginning of the right-hand utility rail. The panel itself has generous internal padding (around 32px on all sides) and a height of roughly 60–70% of the visible viewport, creating a large, immersive card. The background uses the brand’s bold blue-green gradient, with a category logo or mark positioned toward the top-left of the card and a concise headline and supporting copy positioned in the left and central portions. The rightmost quarter of the card’s surface remains relatively clean to avoid visually competing with the vertical utility rail. This creates a strong focal point that mirrors the large hero card in the mockup and sets the tone for the category (e.g., Nutrition Coaching).
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the flow; it scrolls out of view naturally as users move down the page. On larger screens, its height adapts to maintain a visually dominant presence without exceeding approximately 70% of the viewport height.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The nutrition bundle filters appear below the category-selector within the same main left content column. They are separated from the category selector by approximately 32–40px of vertical whitespace, maintaining the spacious editorial rhythm. Horizontally, this filter bar spans the same width as the category selector (about 8–9 grid columns, roughly 70–75% of the container width) and aligns with its left edge. The filters are arranged in a single, wide horizontal band resembling a low-profile card: a keyword input to the left, followed by dropdowns or pill toggles for attributes like duration, intensity, price, or focus. Each field has moderate padding (8–12px vertical, 16px horizontal) and consistent spacing (16–24px) between controls, avoiding any cramped feeling. The right end of this band can taper off into whitespace rather than being fully filled with controls, emphasizing clarity over density. On smaller screens, the filters stack into two or more rows but retain generous vertical spacing between rows.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls with the page and remains part of the main flow; on large screens, it may be set to stick to the top of the viewport (just under the header) after the user scrolls past it, providing persistent filtering without feeling like a heavy sidebar.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The nutrition bundle results list occupies the main visual field beneath the filters and is the core browsing area for bundles. It starts roughly 32–48px below the nutrition-bundle-filters, preserving a clear visual break between filtering and results. Horizontally, it uses the same main left column width: about 8–9 grid columns aligned with the components above, leaving a clean vertical margin to the right where the utility rail and cart access live. Within this area, bundle-card components are arranged either as a single-column stack or a two-column grid depending on screen width. On standard desktop widths, two cards per row are reasonable, with each row’s cards evenly occupying the width and a clear 24–32px gutter between them. Vertically, rows are separated by 24–32px. Ample white space around the results block and between cards keeps the section airy and supports an editorial browsing experience rather than a dense product catalog.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls with the page as a long, vertically expanding section. The layout is responsive, collapsing toward a single column on narrower viewports while keeping side margins proportional to preserve the centered, spacious feeling.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each bundle card is a self-contained, medium-density card within the nutrition-bundle-results area. In desktop view, cards align to the grid so that one or two cards fit per row inside the main left content width. Each card has a white or very light background, subtle corner rounding, and a soft shadow or 1px border to separate it from the page background. Internal padding is generous (approximately 24–32px on all sides), with clear hierarchy: title near the top-left, key metrics or tags (e.g., duration, level) just below, and primary call-to-action and price near the bottom. No card extends into the right utility rail; cards always stay within the left 8–9 grid columns to preserve the cleanliness of the vertical band to the right. Vertical spacing between cards (or rows of cards) is about 24–32px, maintaining the sense of air and avoiding visual overcrowding.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block elements that participate in the results grid; they size fluidly within their column but maintain minimum padding and vertical rhythm. They scroll with the results list and do not have fixed or sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The consultation navigation block is located near the top of the coaching-services-page-root, just below the bottom edge of the global header’s content area. It sits within the centered max-width container and stretches across almost its entire width (about 10–12 grid columns), though the textual/nav content is visually centered or left-centered, leaving comfortable horizontal padding on both sides. Vertically, it is a shallow band—roughly the height of a navigation bar—with 16–24px of padding above and below its items. It can include breadcrumb-like labels or tabs for different consultation modes (e.g., ‘Coaching & Services’, ‘1:1 Consultations’, ‘Corporate’, ‘Teams’). The block has generous whitespace above and below (32–48px above the hero) and uses subtle styling—e.g., a light bottom border or soft background tint—to ensure it is noticeable but not competing with the hero. This positioning makes it the first page-specific element users see after the global nav, guiding context before they engage with the category content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls naturally with the page; on larger displays it can optionally remain just below the fixed header as a secondary sticky nav, but it should maintain a shallow visual weight to preserve the spacious, editorial feel.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart access control lives within a dedicated, narrow vertical utility rail on the right side of the main content area, visually echoing the mockup’s thin vertical band with a partially visible control. This rail occupies approximately 3–4 of the 12 grid columns (about 25–30% of the container width) and runs the full height of the main content, starting at the same vertical level as the category-summary-panel. A subtle vertical divider line separates the rail from the main left content. The cart access control itself is a medium-sized rectangular button or compact card aligned toward the upper-middle portion of this rail when first viewed, with around 24–32px of padding inside and whitespace surrounding it so it does not feel crowded. It might show a cart icon, item count, and a short label (e.g., ‘View Selections’). As the user scrolls down past the hero and into the filters and results, the cart control remains visible by sticking to a position just below the global header within this right-hand rail. This creates a persistent, yet unobtrusive, action anchor that subtly pulls the eye to the right after engaging with the main hero and during browsing of bundles.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Positioned within a right-hand utility rail that scrolls, but the cart control itself becomes sticky within that rail once it reaches a threshold near the top of the viewport (just below the fixed header), ensuring constant access without overlapping main content.

name: Nutrition Bundle Detail
title: Nutrition Bundle Detail
description: Displays detailed information for a selected nutrition coaching bundle and lets users configure options and add it to the cart.

core_features: Read the required bundle_id from the URL query string and load the corresponding nutrition bundle using getNutritionBundleDetail.
Display key bundle attributes such as name, product_type, num_sessions, minutes_per_session, total_minutes, price, and start_window_description.
Present the list of preferred coaches for the bundle (if any) and allow the user to select a preferred coach from a dropdown.
Allow the user to specify a start window in days, aligned with the bundle's start_window_description, for passing to addNutritionBundleToCart.
Show a value summary section that emphasizes total coaching minutes and uses value_summary.cost_per_minute to express perceived value in plain text.
Provide an Add to Cart / Enroll action that calls addNutritionBundleToCart with the selected options and navigates to cart.html on success.
Offer navigation back to the nutrition bundles listing (services.html) with appropriate category and product_type URL parameters.
Handle loading and error states for bundle data and inform the user when the bundle cannot be loaded or added to the cart.
user_workflows: View bundle details: 1) User lands on bundle_detail.html with a bundle_id URL parameter. 2) The page reads bundle_id and calls getNutritionBundleDetail. 3) While loading, a loading state is shown. 4) On success, the page displays the bundle name, key metrics, description, start_window_description, value summary, and any available preferred coaches. 5) If loading fails or the bundle is inactive/unavailable, an error state is shown with a link back to services.html.

Select preferred coach (optional): 1) After the bundle data is loaded, if preferred_coaches is non-empty, the coach dropdown is populated from preferred_coaches. 2) If there is a coach_id present in the URL, the matching coach is preselected when available. 3) The user may change the selected coach, updating the internal selection state. 4) The latest selection is used when calling addNutritionBundleToCart as preferredCoachId.

Specify start window for starting the bundle: 1) After the bundle data is loaded, the start-window selector shows contextual help using bundle.start_window_description. 2) If a start_within_days parameter exists in the URL and is valid, that value is prefilled. 3) The user can adjust the numeric value within allowed validation rules derived from the description or business rules. 4) The final numeric value is used as start_within_days when calling addNutritionBundleToCart.

Add bundle to cart and go to cart: 1) When the user clicks Add to Cart / Enroll, the page validates that bundle_id is present and that the start_within_days value (if required) is valid. 2) The page calls addNutritionBundleToCart with bundleId, preferredCoachId (which may be empty), and start_within_days. 3) While waiting, a submitting state is shown and the button is disabled. 4) On success, a success message is stored in transient state and the user is navigated to cart.html. 5) On failure, an error message from addNutritionBundleToCart.message is shown and the form remains on the same page for correction.

Return to bundles listing: 1) The user clicks the Back to nutrition bundles link in the breadcrumb/navigation component. 2) The page navigates to services.html with category set to nutrition_coaching and product_type set to bundle in the URL query string. 3) The user continues browsing other bundles.

Handle direct deep links and URL changes: 1) If the user or another page changes the bundle_id in the URL query string (for example via navigation), the bundle-detail loader re-executes getNutritionBundleDetail for the new bundleId. 2) Any existing form selections that no longer apply (such as a previously selected coach not present in the new bundle’s preferred_coaches) are reset. 3) The display updates to the newly loaded bundle.
interactions: On initial page load, the page reads bundle_id from the URL query string and triggers getNutritionBundleDetail with that value.
If bundle_id is missing or empty, the page shows an error state and a link to services.html with nutrition_coaching bundle filters applied, without calling getNutritionBundleDetail.
When getNutritionBundleDetail resolves successfully, components bound to bundle, preferred_coaches, and value_summary are rendered or updated.
If getNutritionBundleDetail returns an error or indicates that the bundle is not available, an error message is displayed and the Add to Cart form is disabled.
Changing the selected coach in the coach dropdown updates the preferredCoachId held in the add-to-cart form state.
Editing the start window field updates the start_within_days value in the add-to-cart form state, with validation feedback if the input is invalid.
Clicking the Add to Cart / Enroll button validates the form, calls addNutritionBundleToCart with bundleId, preferredCoachId, and start_within_days, and transitions the button into a loading state.
If addNutritionBundleToCart returns success = true, the page optionally shows a transient success notification and then navigates to cart.html.
If addNutritionBundleToCart returns success = false, the page displays the message field from the response as an error notification and keeps the user on the current page for correction.
Clicking the View Cart link (if provided after adding to cart, or from the main actions) navigates directly to cart.html without calling additional interfaces.
Clicking Back to nutrition bundles navigates the user to services.html with URL parameters category=nutrition_coaching and product_type=bundle.

Changing URL parameters bundle_id, coach_id, or start_within_days (for example via browser navigation or internal links) triggers a re-evaluation of page state: bundle_id causes a new data load; coach_id and start_within_days update initial form selections if compatible with the loaded bundle.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary state of this page is driven by URL query parameters and API responses. The required parameter bundle_id is read on every page load and whenever the URL changes. If bundle_id is present and valid, getNutritionBundleDetail is called with that value as bundleId. The bundle_id value determines which NutritionBundle instance is displayed. Optional URL parameters such as coach_id and start_within_days may be used to pre-populate the preferred coach selection and the start window input in the add-to-cart form. When the loaded bundle changes (because bundle_id changed), previously derived state such as selected coach and start window are recalculated: the selected coach is only preserved if it exists within the returned preferred_coaches; otherwise it is cleared or set to a default valid coach. The start_within_days form value is initialized from the URL when present and valid; if invalid with respect to validation rules or inconsistent with bundle.start_window_description, the field is reset and the user is prompted to choose a valid value. No state is persisted using localStorage or similar mechanisms; instead, the page relies on URL parameters for sharable state, internal in-memory state for transient form entries, and the responses from getNutritionBundleDetail and addNutritionBundleToCart. Navigation to services.html includes category=nutrition_coaching and product_type=bundle in the URL, allowing the listing page to pre-filter. Navigation to cart.html does not require additional parameters for this page’s functionality.

components.functionality: Reads bundle_id from the URL query string, validates it, and calls getNutritionBundleDetail with this value. Manages loading, success, and error states and exposes bundle, preferred_coaches, and value_summary data to child components.

Provides navigation controls including a link back to the nutrition bundles listing on services.html with appropriate category and product_type URL parameters and a non-clickable label for the current bundle.
Displays the bundle name and core identifiers (such as product_type) so the user can confirm they are viewing the correct nutrition bundle.

Shows the key numeric metrics for the bundle, including num_sessions, minutes_per_session, total_minutes, price, start_window_description, and a textual explanation of value using value_summary.cost_per_minute without letting users run their own calculations.
Displays the long-form description of the nutrition bundle to inform users about the coaching content, structure, and who it is suitable for.

If preferred coaches are available for the bundle, renders a dropdown list populated from preferred_coaches, allows the user to select a preferred coach, and stores the selected coach ID for use in the add-to-cart call.

Provides an input (for example, numeric) where the user specifies the number of days within which they plan to start the bundle. Uses bundle.start_window_description to give contextual help and validation rules. The resulting numeric value is used as start_within_days for addNutritionBundleToCart.

Coordinates selections from coach-selector and start-window-selector, validates that the configuration is complete and valid, and when the user clicks Add to Cart / Enroll, calls addNutritionBundleToCart with bundleId, preferredCoachId, and start_within_days. Handles success and error responses and triggers navigation to cart.html upon a successful add.
Displays status messages and feedback to the user, including loading errors from getNutritionBundleDetail and success or error messages from addNutritionBundleToCart.

layout.grid_system: Visually consistent with a 12-column responsive grid centered in the viewport, with a max-width content container and substantial left/right margins.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows a strictly top-to-bottom, single-column content flow inside a centered max-width container. Above it, the global utility bar and fixed main header (logo left, centered nav) remain consistent with the mockup. Within the Nutrition Bundle Detail page itself, the first element in the content column is a slim breadcrumb row, followed by a dominant hero-like bundle card that occupies most of the initial viewport height. Inside this hero card, content is split into a wider left content column (bundle title, key metrics) and a narrower right configuration column (selectors and add-to-cart). Below the hero, secondary content such as the full bundle description continues in stacked sections. Notification feedback stays close to the configuration area to preserve the linear narrative from selection to purchase.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows a strictly top-to-bottom, single-column content flow inside a centered max-width container. Above it, the global utility bar and fixed main header (logo left, centered nav) remain consistent with the mockup. Within the Nutrition Bundle Detail page itself, the first element in the content column is a slim breadcrumb row, followed by a dominant hero-like bundle card that occupies most of the initial viewport height. Inside this hero card, content is split into a wider left content column (bundle title, key metrics) and a narrower right configuration column (selectors and add-to-cart). Below the hero, secondary content such as the full bundle description continues in stacked sections. Notification feedback stays close to the configuration area to preserve the linear narrative from selection to purchase.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The breadcrumb uses minimal vertical space, acting as a lightweight locator. The hero bundle card is the primary spatial anchor on the page, taking roughly 60–70% of the viewport height on desktop and spanning about 80–90% of the content container width, centered. Inside this card, about 70–75% of the card width is reserved for the left content column and 25–30% for the right configuration band, separated by a subtle vertical divider. Generous vertical margins (~48–64px) separate the hero from the following description section, and similar spacing is maintained between sections below. Internal padding within cards and sections remains around 24–32px on all sides to match the mockup's editorial spaciousness.
layout_strategies.content_density: The layout keeps density low and controlled. Above the fold, only the breadcrumb and a single, visually strong hero card are prominent. The left side of the hero focuses on minimal, high-impact information: the bundle name and a concise metric summary. The right side presents only the essential configuration fields and a single, dominant CTA, each with ample breathing room. Detailed text (full description) is pushed below the hero into its own section so the initial view feels clean and professional, in line with the consultancy/wellbeing positioning.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Visual flow mirrors the mockup’s F-shaped pattern. Users first notice the global logo and centered navigation in the fixed header, then drop down into the page content at the top-left via the breadcrumb. From there, the eye is pulled down and slightly right to the large color-backed hero card. Within the hero, the gaze starts at the upper-left with the bundle header, then moves slightly downward to the metrics panel. The eye is then drawn horizontally to the right-hand configuration band and the add-to-cart button, aided by the vertical divider. Below the fold, the flow continues down into the full description section. Any status/feedback banner sits close to the CTA, keeping user attention anchored around actions and outcomes.

layout.overall_description: Below the fixed global header, the Nutrition Bundle Detail page opens with a slim breadcrumb trail aligned to the left within a centered, max-width container, establishing context without drawing much visual weight. A generous vertical gap leads into a large, card-like hero band with a subtle blue gradient or brand-colored background that dominates the first screen. This hero card is centered, with wide left and right margins, and internally split into two vertical zones: a broader left column containing the bundle name and key performance or value metrics, and a narrow right band defined by a subtle vertical divider that houses the coach selection, start window field, and add-to-cart controls. The right band feels like a vertical control rail, echoing the carousel-control area in the mockup, but dedicated here to purchase configuration. The main content is airy; there’s plenty of whitespace above, between, and below elements so that the hero remains the clear focal point. As users scroll, they move into a more text-oriented, white-background description section that spans most of the container width but remains comfortably padded and visually calm. Any loading or feedback overlays float above this structure but are restrained in size and style, preserving the clean, corporate look while clearly signaling system state.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The data loader appears as a semi-translucent overlay positioned above the entire main content area (from just below the fixed site header to the bottom of the viewport). It covers the hero card and subsequent sections with a soft white or very light gray veil at around 60–70% opacity. At the exact center of the viewport, a small to medium-sized spinner or progress indicator sits within a subtle rounded card (e.g., 240–280px wide) that uses internal padding of about 24px and a faint shadow. This central loader card is horizontally centered and vertically aligned to the middle of the visible area, ensuring it is immediately visible without feeling heavy or blocking the perception of the underlying layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed overlay within the viewport when active, covering the scrollable content while allowing the global header to remain visually distinguishable on top.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The breadcrumb navigation sits at the top of the page’s content column, directly beneath the global header and utility bar, aligned flush to the left edge of the max-width container. It spans the full width of this container horizontally but is visually compact in height, with about 8–12px of vertical padding above and below the breadcrumb text. There is a comfortable gap of roughly 24–32px between the bottom of the breadcrumbs and the top edge of the hero bundle card, reinforcing the sense of hierarchy and allowing the hero card to breathe.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, scrolling naturally with the page content as part of the main content flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The bundle header forms the top-left anchor of the large hero card. The hero card itself is centered within the page content area, occupying about 80–90% of the max-width container and roughly 60–70% of the viewport height on desktop. Inside this card, the bundle header is placed in the upper-left quadrant, starting with about 32px of padding from the card’s top and left edges. It spans approximately 70–75% of the card’s internal width (the full left content region) and defines a strong horizontal line that the eye first lands on. The header includes the main bundle title and a short supporting line, stacked with 8–16px of vertical gap between them. There is around 16–24px of vertical space between the bottom of the header and the top of the bundle-metrics-panel, preserving an editorial rhythm while still keeping these elements visually grouped.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element within the hero card, maintaining its relative position as the user scrolls past the hero.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The bundle metrics panel is positioned directly beneath the bundle header within the same left-hand content column of the hero card. It aligns to the same left inset as the header (around 32px from the card’s left edge) and spans the full width of the left column, which is about 70–75% of the card’s internal width. Vertically, it appears as a horizontal band taking up perhaps 20–25% of the hero card’s height, with 24–32px of internal padding separating the metric items from the card background. Metrics are laid out in a row, each occupying an equal fraction (e.g., 3 or 4 columns in the 12-column grid), with 16–24px of horizontal gap between them, creating a clean strip of key values. There is a comfortable 24–32px gap beneath the metrics panel before any subsequent visual content, ensuring the panel reads as a discrete summary block within the hero.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the hero card, expanding vertically if additional metrics are present but always constrained to the left content area.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The bundle description panel is placed immediately below the hero card as the first major section in the stacked flow after the hero. It sits within the same centered max-width container and is aligned to the left edge of that container. The top of this section begins approximately 48–64px below the bottom edge of the hero card, maintaining the generous inter-section spacing seen in the mockup. The description content itself is housed within a white or very light background card or section with 24–32px of internal padding on all sides. On desktop, the text column occupies around 70–80% of the container width, aligned left, leaving a comfortable margin on the right to preserve the page’s airy feel. The panel may extend vertically as needed for long-form text, but line length remains controlled to a readable width, echoing the mockup’s editorial tone.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static content section, scrolling naturally as part of the vertical narrative beneath the hero.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The coach selector appears inside the hero card within a dedicated right-hand configuration band. This band occupies roughly 25–30% of the hero card’s internal width and is visually separated from the left content by a thin vertical divider line running from near the top to near the bottom of the card. The band begins around 32px from the card’s top edge, aligning vertically with the bundle header. Within this band, the coach selector is the first interactive control, positioned near the top with about 24px of padding from the top and right edges of the band. It spans the full internal width of the band (minus 16–24px horizontal padding on each side), appearing as a clean, single-line dropdown with its label above it. There is a 16–24px vertical gap below the coach selector before the start-window-selector, keeping the configuration steps visually ordered and uncluttered.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right configuration column, maintaining alignment with the hero’s top content on scroll.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The start window selector is positioned directly below the coach selector in the same right-hand configuration band of the hero card. It inherits the same horizontal alignment and width, filling the available band width with standard 16–24px side padding. Vertically, there is a clear separation of about 16–24px between the bottom of the coach selector and the label of the start window selector, making the sequence of fields feel stepwise. The element itself is concise—a date input or short form control—so it uses relatively little vertical height, preserving the light, low-density feeling of the configuration area. Another 24px gap is maintained beneath this selector before the top edge of the add-to-cart form, visually grouping all configuration-related controls within the right column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static control within the right configuration column, expanding slightly vertically if helper text or validation messages appear.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The bundle add-to-cart form sits at the bottom of the right-hand configuration band within the hero card, directly beneath the start-window-selector. It uses the same internal width as the selectors, stretching from the divider line to the right padding edge of the band. A vertical gap of approximately 24px separates it from the start-window-selector above, giving the CTA some breathing room. The main button (e.g., “Add to cart”) is horizontally full-width within this band, with 12–16px vertical padding and 16–24px side padding inside the button, making it the strongest focal element in the configuration column. If price or quantity information is shown, it appears immediately above or to the left of the button, but still within the confines of this right band. The bottom of the band has about 24–32px of internal padding below the button, ensuring the CTA doesn’t feel crammed against the card edge.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right configuration column; may grow slightly vertically to accommodate form validation or secondary text, pushing the bottom of the hero card down as needed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The status and feedback banner is positioned within the same right-hand configuration band as the selectors and add-to-cart form, appearing immediately below the add-to-cart form area. When present, it occupies the full internal width of this band, aligned to both the divider on the left and the band’s right padding edge. A small vertical gap of about 16px separates it from the bottom of the add-to-cart form, maintaining a clear relationship between the action and its feedback. The banner is relatively shallow in height, with 12–16px of vertical padding and 16–24px of horizontal padding inside, and may use a subtle colored background (e.g., soft green or red) to indicate success or error while still fitting the conservative corporate aesthetic. If multiple lines of feedback are needed, the banner expands downward within the hero card, pushing its bottom edge down slightly but staying spatially linked to the configuration area rather than spanning the full hero width.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right configuration column; appears and disappears dynamically based on state, shifting surrounding vertical spacing but not altering the overall column structure.

name: Challenges Listing
title: Challenges
description: Page for browsing, filtering, and selecting short-term wellbeing and movement challenges, with support for free and paid options and quick access to a 7-day style challenge.

core_features: Load available filter options for challenges (price types and durations) using getChallengeFilterOptions and expose them as interactive filters.
List challenges using searchChallenges with active filters (price_type, duration_days) and pagination parameters.
Allow users to filter challenges by price type, including showing only free challenges when requested.
Allow users to filter challenges by duration in days, including quickly focusing on 7-day-style challenges when requested.
Surface a clearly identifiable featured challenge entry based on a 7-day-style filter (or the incoming duration_days) for quick selection.
Enable navigation from any challenge entry to its detail page (challenge_detail.html) using challenge_id.
Provide a cart navigation control to cart.html for users who have added or intend to review paid challenges on other pages.
user_workflows:
Browse all challenges from header navigation:
1. User clicks the 'Challenges' link in the header which opens challenges.html with no URL parameters.
2. Page calls getChallengeFilterOptions to retrieve available price_types and durations_days.
3. Page derives default filter state (e.g., no price_type filter and no duration_days filter initially) and sets page = 1.
4. Page calls searchChallenges with the current filters and pagination.
5. The list of challenges is rendered in the challenges list/grid component.
6. User optionally adjusts filters (price type, duration), which updates the URL parameters and re-calls searchChallenges.
7. User clicks a challenge entry to navigate to challenge_detail.html?challenge_id={challenge_id}.

Landing with preselected free 7-day-style challenges from index:
1. User clicks a link on index.html that navigates to challenges.html with URL parameters such as price_type=free and duration_days=7.
2. On load, the page reads price_type and duration_days from the URL and sets them as the initial filter state.
3. Page calls getChallengeFilterOptions to populate filter controls and marks the corresponding filter options as selected based on URL parameters.
4. Page calls searchChallenges with filters including price_type=free and duration_days=7 and page = 1.
5. Results are rendered; a featured-challenge component highlights one of the matching challenges as a quick-access option.
6. User can either select the featured challenge (navigating to its detail page) or scroll the list to review other matching challenges.
7. User may change filters (e.g., removing duration_days) which updates the URL and refreshes results via searchChallenges.

Switching between free and paid challenges:
1. User opens challenges.html (with or without initial URL parameters).
2. User selects a price-type filter (e.g., free or paid) in the filter bar.
3. The filter bar component updates the internal filter state and price_type URL parameter, resets page to 1, and triggers searchChallenges with the updated filters.
4. The result list and featured challenge update accordingly.
5. User can clear the price-type filter, which removes price_type from the URL and re-runs searchChallenges without that filter.

Refining by duration and paging through results:
1. User opens challenges.html and sets a duration_days filter via the duration filter control (e.g., selecting one of the durations returned by getChallengeFilterOptions).
2. The filter bar updates the internal filter state, updates duration_days in the URL, resets page to 1, and triggers searchChallenges.
3. If there are more results than fit on a single page_size, pagination controls are enabled using searchChallenges.total, .page, and .page_size.
4. User clicks a pagination control (next, previous, or a page number), which updates the page URL parameter and calls searchChallenges with the same filters but a different page.
5. The list and featured challenge (if configured to follow the current filters) update to show the corresponding page of results.

Navigating to cart from challenges listing:
1. User is browsing challenges and sees a cart icon/button in the header or within the page chrome.
2. User clicks the cart icon.
3. Browser navigates to cart.html (e.g., cart.html) without modifying challenge-related filters.
4. When user navigates back to challenges.html (via browser back or navigation), the page reads URL parameters again and re-runs getChallengeFilterOptions and searchChallenges to restore the filtered view.
interactions: On page load, read URL parameters (price_type, duration_days, page) and initialize the filter state, then call getChallengeFilterOptions and searchChallenges.
User changes the price-type filter (e.g., selects 'free' or 'paid'); the filter bar updates the URL price_type parameter, resets the page parameter to 1, and triggers a new searchChallenges call.
User changes the duration filter (e.g., selects a duration in days); the filter bar updates the URL duration_days parameter, resets the page parameter to 1, and triggers a new searchChallenges call.
User clears one or all filters (e.g., via a 'clear filters' control); the filter bar removes the corresponding URL parameters and triggers searchChallenges with the reduced filter set.
User clicks a featured challenge entry; the featured component navigates to challenge_detail.html?challenge_id={challenge_id}.
User clicks any challenge card in the main listing; the listing component navigates to challenge_detail.html?challenge_id={challenge_id}.
User interacts with pagination controls; the pagination component updates the page parameter in the URL and triggers searchChallenges with the new page.
User clicks the cart icon; the navigation component takes the user to cart.html.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The challenges listing state is driven only by URL parameters and API responses, not by localStorage.

URL parameters:
- price_type (string, optional): When present (e.g., 'free' or 'paid'), it is included in the filters object passed to searchChallenges. It also determines which price-type filter option is preselected in the UI. When the user changes the price-type filter, the page updates this parameter (or removes it when cleared) and triggers a new search.
- duration_days (number, optional): When present, it is parsed as a number and included in the filters object passed to searchChallenges. The duration filter control preselects this duration where possible. If the user changes or clears the duration filter, the URL duration_days is updated or removed and searchChallenges is called again.
- page (number, optional): Represents the current results page for pagination. If absent or invalid, it defaults to the first page as defined by the application configuration. When pagination controls are used, this parameter is updated and searchChallenges is called with the new page while preserving the current filters.

State derivation and propagation:
1. On initial load, the page reads price_type, duration_days, and page from the URL to form the initial filter state.
2. The page calls getChallengeFilterOptions to retrieve available price types and duration options; this data initializes the filter controls. If the incoming URL values do not match any available options, the UI may show them as custom selections while still passing them through to searchChallenges.
3. The page then calls searchChallenges with an object like { filters: { price_type: <from URL or null>, duration_days: <from URL or null> }, page: <from URL or default>, page_size: <from app configuration> }.
4. The searchChallenges response determines the rendered challenge list, pagination controls (based on total, page, page_size), and candidate items for the featured challenge component.
5. Any user interaction that modifies filters or the current page triggers both an update to the URL parameters (via history push/replace) and a new call to searchChallenges using the updated state. This ensures that reloading or sharing the URL restores the same filtered list and page.
6. The featured challenge component derives its state from the current filters and searchChallenges results. For example, when duration_days is set (or when the application wants to emphasize 7-day-style challenges), it selects an appropriate challenge from the current result set or by issuing a dedicated searchChallenges call with duration_days=7 and optionally price_type=free (if that aligns with the incoming filters), without hardcoding any specific challenge IDs or static dataset rows.

components.functionality: Initializes the challenges listing page by reading URL parameters, fetching filter options, constructing the initial filters object, and triggering the first searchChallenges call.
Displays and manages filter controls for price type and duration, populated via getChallengeFilterOptions. Updates URL parameters and triggers searchChallenges when filters change.

Highlights a single challenge for quick selection based on current filters and/or a 7-day-style focus. Uses searchChallenges results or a targeted searchChallenges call with duration_days (e.g., 7) and an optional price_type to identify a candidate challenge, then exposes it as a prominent, clickable entry.
data_binding: searchChallenges.response.results (as the primary source of candidate challenges)
searchChallenges.targeted.response.results (optional dedicated call when no suitable candidate is found in the main results)

components.functionality: Renders the list or grid of challenge entries returned from searchChallenges, showing key attributes such as name, duration_days, focus, price_type, and price, and enabling navigation to challenge_detail.html on selection.
data_binding: searchChallenges.response.results (each item corresponding to a Challenge entity subset)

components.functionality: Displays pagination controls based on searchChallenges.total, searchChallenges.page, and searchChallenges.page_size, and allows the user to navigate between pages of challenge results.
Shows a functional message or guidance when searchChallenges.response.results is empty for the current filters and offers a control to clear or adjust filters.
Indicates when the page is actively waiting for getChallengeFilterOptions or searchChallenges responses, based on pending request state.
Provides a control (e.g., an icon or button) that navigates the user to cart.html for reviewing or finalizing any paid challenge purchases initiated elsewhere in the site.

layout.grid_system: Visually consistent with a 12-column responsive grid centered in the viewport, with a max-width content container and substantial left/right margins.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the mockup’s linear, top-to-bottom structure inside an existing shell: a fixed global utility bar and header at the very top; below that, a centered main content column. Inside this column, the challenges page starts with a shallow filter bar, then a dominant featured challenge hero panel that mirrors the mockup’s full-width card with a right-hand control strip. Beneath the hero, a stacked challenges grid fills the main content width, followed by centered pagination. Empty and loading states occupy the same main content region, replacing or overlaying the grid/pagination without changing the overall structure.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the mockup’s linear, top-to-bottom structure inside an existing shell: a fixed global utility bar and header at the very top; below that, a centered main content column. Inside this column, the challenges page starts with a shallow filter bar, then a dominant featured challenge hero panel that mirrors the mockup’s full-width card with a right-hand control strip. Beneath the hero, a stacked challenges grid fills the main content width, followed by centered pagination. Empty and loading states occupy the same main content region, replacing or overlaying the grid/pagination without changing the overall structure.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The layout uses a max-width, centered container on a 12-column grid. The filter bar is a low-height band spanning nearly the full container width. The featured challenge hero panel consumes most of the visible fold area: roughly 60–70% of the viewport height and about 70% of the container width, aligned to the left, with the remaining 30% reserved as a narrow, light right-hand band containing the slider/carousel control. The challenges grid then uses the full container width, with generous gutters between cards. Vertical spacing between filter bar, hero, grid, and pagination follows the larger scale (48–64px) to keep the page airy and editorial. The cart icon is compact and lives in the header’s right edge without affecting the main column.
layout_strategies.content_density: The density matches the mockup’s low, editorial feel. The filter bar exposes only essential controls in a single horizontal row with breathing room between elements. The featured hero panel is visually dominant but light on text, focusing on a bold gradient, logo/mark, a clear title, a short description, and one primary action. The challenges grid is moderately dense (e.g., 2–3 cards per row on desktop) but each card has ample internal padding and whitespace to avoid feeling crowded. Empty and loading states appear clean and minimal, with a single icon/illustration, a short message, and maybe one supporting action.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye starts at the global logo in the fixed header, moves across the centered navigation, then to the cart icon at the top-right. Scrolling slightly, attention drops to the filter bar (functional controls), then is drawn strongly into the bold blue-green featured hero card on the left. Within that hero, the user’s gaze goes from the logo mark to the main headline and CTA, then shifts right to the slim vertical divider and partially visible carousel control, hinting at horizontal browsing of featured challenges. From there, the eye naturally moves downward into the vertically stacked challenges grid and finally to the pagination at the bottom center.

layout.overall_description: Within a clean, fixed header shell, the challenges page sits in a centered, max-width container that feels spacious and controlled. Immediately below the header, a shallow, full-width filter bar stretches across the main column, giving users a clear, functional starting point. A generous vertical gap separates this bar from a large featured challenge hero panel that mirrors the mockup’s bold card: a blue gradient tile anchored to the left two-thirds of the content area, with a slim, light right-hand strip separated by a vertical divider and containing a partially visible slider control. This hero occupies much of the initial viewport height, surrounded by white space above and below. Underneath, the layout shifts to a more utilitarian but still airy grid of challenge cards spanning the full container width, arranged in two or three columns with comfortable gutters. At the bottom of the content stack, pagination controls are centered with ample top and bottom margins so the page ends in a calm, orderly way. When no results or loading states occur, they take over the main content region beneath the filter bar and hero, remaining centered and minimal to preserve the overall corporate, editorial feel.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component is non-visual and conceptually sits at the top of the main content region, immediately below the fixed global header and utility bar and above the filter bar. It occupies no visible space in the layout; instead, it initializes state and data for the entire page. Spatially, it can be thought of as anchored to the left edge of the centered main content container, spanning its full width but with zero height, so all following components (filter bar, hero, list) are arranged directly beneath it in the vertical flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-rendered; participates only in logical ordering of components, not in visual layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The filter bar is the first visible element of the challenges page content, positioned directly beneath the global header and utility bar, and anchored within the centered max-width container. It runs horizontally across approximately 90–100% of the content container width, aligned with the same left and right margins as the hero panel and grid. Vertically, it is a shallow band—roughly 64–80px tall—with 16–24px of internal padding top and bottom. Filter controls (e.g., dropdowns, pills, a search field, free/paid toggles) are arranged in a single horizontal row, starting at the left edge of the content column and flowing to the right with consistent gaps following the 8–16px step of the spacing scale. There is generous white space above (about 24–32px from the bottom of the header) and below (about 32–48px before the featured hero) to maintain the airy, editorial feel and clearly separate navigation from page-specific controls.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls normally with the page as part of the main content; aligned to the same grid as the hero and list for a consistent column structure.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The featured challenge panel is the primary visual anchor of the page and sits directly below the filter bar, separated by a generous vertical gap of about 48px. Within the centered max-width container, the panel is a large, horizontally oriented card that follows the mockup’s hero pattern. On desktop, the hero card itself occupies roughly 70% of the container width on the left (about 8 of 12 grid columns), with the remaining 30% on the right forming a lighter, narrow strip. The entire hero block (card plus right strip) is about 60–70% of the viewport height, but never taller than needed to keep content comfortably above the fold. The main card portion has a bold blue-green gradient background, rounded corners, and 24–32px internal padding on all sides. Inside the card, content is arranged in a loose vertical stack: a small logo or icon near the top-left, followed by a prominent challenge title, a short supporting line of text, and a primary CTA button near the bottom-left. All content is aligned with the overall grid, so text sits roughly within the left 50–60% of the card width, preserving some breathing space on the right side of the card. Adjacent to this, on the right, a vertical divider line runs the full height of the hero block between the card and the narrow right band. This right band uses a light background (e.g., off-white) and contains a rectangular carousel control aligned vertically center-right, partially clipped by the container’s right edge so it looks like there are more featured challenges to slide through. This control is subtly sized—about 25–30% of the hero’s height and 40–50% of the right band’s width—and indicates horizontal navigation (arrows or thumbnail). The entire hero block is centered horizontally in the viewport but visually weighted to the left, mirroring the mockup’s card-with-right-control composition.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls with the page; participates in the main vertical flow. The hero’s internal content and right-hand control adjust responsively (e.g., stack vertically on small screens, with the right band moving below the main card).

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The challenges list appears below the featured hero panel, separated by a generous vertical gap of about 48–64px to clearly distinguish the editorial-style hero from the more utilitarian listing. It is contained within the same centered max-width column and respects the 12-column grid. On desktop, the list renders as a grid of challenge cards spanning the full content width (all 12 columns), typically in 2–3 cards per row: for example, three cards each occupying about 3–4 grid columns (roughly 30% of container width) with 24–32px horizontal gutters. Each card has generous internal padding (~24px), a light background, and is separated vertically by 24–32px, maintaining the overall low-density, breathable feel. The leftmost column of cards aligns vertically with the left edge of the hero card above, so the main reading line remains straight. The grid extends downward as needed, pushing pagination below; there is at least 48px of clear space between the bottom edge of the hero block and the top row of cards to preserve the hero’s prominence.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls with the page and expands vertically as more challenge cards are present. On smaller screens, the grid collapses to two columns, then to a single column on mobile, while maintaining consistent left/right margins and vertical spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pagination controls are positioned directly below the challenges list, separated from the last row of cards by about 32–48px of vertical space. They are centered horizontally within the same max-width container, creating a clear visual end to the current set of results. The pagination bar itself is compact—roughly one line tall—with numbered page buttons and previous/next controls laid out in a horizontal row. Each pagination button has 8–12px vertical and 16–24px horizontal padding, echoing the navigation/link padding from the design DNA, and they are spaced apart with 8–16px gaps. There is additional whitespace (about 48–64px) below the pagination before the bottom of the viewport or next section, reinforcing the sense of closure and maintaining the airy, corporate feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls normally; remains at the bottom of the challenges list and does not stick to the viewport. It repositions vertically as the number of challenge rows changes.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The empty state indicator occupies the main content area where the challenges grid and pagination would normally appear, starting just below the featured hero panel (or directly below the filter bar if the hero is not shown for that context). It is centered both horizontally and, where possible, vertically within the available space, while still respecting the same left and right margins of the main content container. The empty state consists of a modest illustration or icon at the top center, a short, clear message beneath it, and optionally a single primary or secondary action (e.g., “Reset filters”) below the text. The content is arranged in a tight vertical stack with 16–24px spacing between elements, but the block as a whole is surrounded by generous whitespace—at least 64px from the hero above and from the bottom edge of the viewport if content is short. No challenge cards or pagination are shown when this component is visible, keeping the layout calm and uncluttered.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Replaces the challenges list and pagination in the vertical flow when active; scrolls normally and does not overlay the hero or filter bar. Its size adjusts to the viewport, remaining centered as the window resizes.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loading indicator appears over the main content region when data for the challenges list is being fetched. Visually, it is centered within the same max-width container and typically sits around the vertical midpoint of the visible main area beneath the filter bar and hero. To maintain the underlying layout structure, the loading indicator can be implemented as a semi-transparent overlay layer: a light, subtle scrim covers the area where the hero, list, and pagination would be, with a spinner or progress indicator and a short loading label positioned at the exact center of the content column. The header, utility bar, and cart icon remain fully visible and interactive at the top. The overlay leaves 24–32px padding from the container edges, mirroring the card padding and preserving the sense of a clean, controlled layout even while loading.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Temporarily overlays the hero, challenges list, and pagination within the main content container while loading, without affecting the fixed global header. It is removed once content loads, restoring full interaction with underlying components.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart navigation icon is located in the global header band, anchored to the far right of the header’s max-width content container. Horizontally, it sits on the same baseline as the centered navigation links, aligned with the right margin of the grid so that the logo is on the far left, navigation is centered, and the cart icon visually balances the header on the right. The icon itself is compact—approximately 24–32px in size—with a touch target padded to about 40–48px square, matching the navigation item height. There is at least 16–24px of horizontal spacing between the rightmost navigation link and the cart icon to prevent visual crowding. Vertically, it is centered within the header’s height, with consistent top and bottom padding mirroring the nav. The icon remains visible on all pages, including the challenges listing, maintaining a clear, persistent entry point to the cart.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed indirectly via the header: it moves with the header, which is fixed to the top of the viewport. The cart icon does not scroll with the main content; it stays in place at the top-right as users scroll through the challenges page.

name: Challenge Detail
title: Challenge Detail
description: Detail and registration page for a specific challenge, showing dynamic challenge information and allowing users to join with their preferred start date, time, and themes.

core_features: Read the challenge_id from the URL query string and load the corresponding challenge information using getChallengeDetail.
Display challenge details, including name, description, duration, focus, price information, and delivery channel, based on the getChallengeDetail response.
Show a dynamic list of available themes for the challenge (labels and codes) using the available_themes array from getChallengeDetail.
Provide a 'Join Now' / 'Sign Up' call-to-action that moves focus to the registration form.
Render a registration form that collects first_name, email, start_date, preferred_email_time, and selected_themes (mapped from available_themes).
Validate required registration fields on the client before submission (presence and format where applicable).
Submit the registration via registerForChallenge, using the current challengeId and user-entered values.
Show a confirmation state after a successful registration, using the data returned from registerForChallenge (including start_date, preferred_email_time, selected_themes, and message).
Provide navigation back to the overall challenges listing (challenges.html), passing the current challenge’s price_type as a URL parameter.
Provide navigation back to the site home (index.html) from the registration confirmation state.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: View a challenge and decide to join
1) User opens challenge_detail.html with a challenge_id parameter in the query string.
2) The page initializer reads challenge_id from the URL and calls getChallengeDetail with that value.
3) While data is loading, the page shows a loading state.
4) When getChallengeDetail returns, the page displays the challenge name, duration, focus, price information, delivery_channel, and description.
5) The page also lists all available themes from available_themes, so the user can see what aspects the challenge can cover.
6) The user clicks the primary call-to-action button (e.g., 'Join Now'), which scrolls the page to the registration form and focuses the first input field.

Workflow 2: Register for the challenge
1) After viewing the details, the user fills in the registration form: first_name, email, start_date, preferred_email_time, and selects one or more themes from the dynamically loaded available_themes list (or leaves them unselected if themes are optional).
2) The user clicks the submit button on the registration form (e.g., 'Join Challenge' or 'Start Challenge').
3) The form component performs client-side validation: ensuring first_name, email, start_date, preferred_email_time are present and that email and preferred_email_time follow expected formats.
4) If validation fails, the form shows inline errors and does not call registerForChallenge.
5) If validation passes, the form calls registerForChallenge with challengeId (from URL), first_name, email, start_date, preferred_email_time, and selected_themes (as an array of theme_code values from the checkboxes).
6) While the registration request is in progress, the form shows a submitting state and disables further input.
7) When registerForChallenge returns with success = true, the page stores the response payload in local component state and switches to a confirmation view.
8) If registerForChallenge returns success = false or an error occurs, the form shows an error state using the returned message if available.

Workflow 3: After registration - confirmation and navigation
1) On successful registration, the confirmation component displays: the challenge_name, the confirmed start_date, preferred_email_time, selected_themes, and any message returned by registerForChallenge.
2) The confirmation component also shows a button or link to return to the home page (index.html).
3) The confirmation component may also provide a link back to the list of challenges (challenges.html). This link is constructed with a price_type query parameter, whose value is taken from the loaded challenge.price_type.
4) If the user clicks 'Return to Home', the page navigates to index.html.
5) If the user clicks 'Back to all challenges', the page navigates to challenges.html with the price_type parameter set based on the current challenge’s price_type.

Workflow 4: Handling missing or invalid challenge_id
1) If the page loads without a challenge_id in the URL, the initializer detects the missing parameter.
2) The page shows an error state indicating that no challenge could be loaded and prevents the registration form from being used.
3) The error state offers navigation options: a link back to challenges.html (without any filters) and a link to index.html.
4) If challenge_id is present but getChallengeDetail returns an error (e.g., challenge not found or inactive), the page shows a similar error state instead of the detail and registration form.
interactions: On page load: The initializer component reads the challenge_id query parameter and triggers getChallengeDetail. If challenge_id is absent, it enters an error state without calling the API.

On getChallengeDetail success: The challenge detail components bind to the returned challenge object and available_themes array, populating all descriptive fields and theme selection options. The registration form’s theme checkboxes are generated from available_themes and use their theme_code values as the underlying form values.
On getChallengeDetail error: The page displays a generic error message and disables or hides the registration form, while still showing navigation options to challenges.html and index.html.

On 'Join Now' button click: The browser scrolls the viewport to the registration form component and focuses the first_name input (or the first invalid field if there are validation errors present from a previous attempt).

On registration form field input/change: The form’s internal state is updated to reflect current values for first_name, email, start_date, preferred_email_time, and selected_themes. Any validation messages for a field are cleared or updated when that field changes.

On theme checkbox toggle: The selected_themes array in the form state is updated by adding or removing the corresponding theme_code provided by available_themes. The labels displayed to the user come from the label field in each available_themes entry.

On registration form submit: The form runs validation. If validation fails, it sets appropriate error flags for each field and prevents the API call. If validation passes, it calls registerForChallenge with the parameters bound from the form fields and the challengeId derived from challenge_id in the URL.

On registerForChallenge success: The submission state is cleared, the response object is stored in the confirmation component’s state, and the page switches from the editable form view to the confirmation view (within the same page). The form may optionally be hidden or collapsed.

On registerForChallenge failure: The form displays an error message using the message property from the API response when provided, or a generic error message otherwise. The submit button becomes active again so the user can retry.
On 'Back to all challenges' click: The page navigates to challenges.html with a price_type query parameter whose value is derived from challenge.price_type returned by getChallengeDetail.
On 'Return to Home' click: The page navigates to index.html without additional query parameters.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary state driver is the challenge_id URL query parameter on challenge_detail.html. On initial load, the page reads challenge_id from the query string. If challenge_id is present, getChallengeDetail is called with that value to retrieve the challenge object and available_themes. If challenge_id is missing, the page enters an error state and does not attempt to load or register for a challenge. The page may also use an optional view parameter in the URL to control whether the registration form or confirmation is the default visible section (for example, view=form or view=confirmation). This parameter is read on load and determines which component is initially visible. Regardless of the view parameter, getChallengeDetail is always called when challenge_id is valid, so that navigation actions (such as 'Back to all challenges' which needs challenge.price_type) can be correctly configured. Registration state (success, registration_id, and details) is primarily managed in in-memory component state based on the registerForChallenge response. The URL does not need to store the registration details themselves; instead, the presence of a view value indicating confirmation can be used to show the confirmation portion of the UI after successful registration or when the page is reloaded with that state. When navigating away (to challenges.html or index.html), the page constructs destination URLs using appropriate query parameters, specifically adding price_type to the challenges.html URL using the current challenge’s price_type value, without hardcoding any specific price_type.

components.functionality: On page load, parse the challenge_id and optional view parameters from the URL query string, validate challenge_id presence, call getChallengeDetail with challengeId when valid, and manage global loading/error state for the page.
event_handlers: onLoad: read challenge_id and view from URL
onLoad: if challenge_id exists, call getChallengeDetail(challengeId)
onLoad: if challenge_id missing, set page state to 'error-no-challenge-id'
onGetChallengeDetailSuccess: store challenge and available_themes in shared page state

components.functionality: Display the core challenge metadata returned by getChallengeDetail, such as name, duration_days, focus, price_type, price, and delivery_channel, and provide the main 'Join Now' call-to-action that moves the user to the registration form.
event_handlers: onClickJoinNow: focus first field of challenge-registration-form

components.functionality: Show the full challenge.description text and, if needed, split or structure it into readable paragraphs. This component is purely descriptive and read-only.

Show the list of available themes for the challenge based on the available_themes array. This component is informational (e.g., 'What can this challenge focus on?') and also feeds the theme options used by the registration form.

Collect participant details (first_name, email), start_date, preferred_email_time, and selected_themes. Provide validation, submit the data through registerForChallenge, and expose submission status and results to the confirmation component.
data_binding: registerForChallenge (output used to set registrationResult state on success or failure)
event_handlers: onFieldChange:first_name: update internal form state and clear related validation errors
onFieldChange:email: update internal form state and clear related validation errors
onFieldChange:start_date: update internal form state and clear related validation errors
onFieldChange:preferred_email_time: update internal form state and clear related validation errors
onThemeCheckboxToggle: update internal selected_themes array by adding/removing the theme_code value
onSubmit: validate required fields and formats
onSubmit: if validation passes, call registerForChallenge({ challengeId, first_name, email, start_date, preferred_email_time, selected_themes })
onSubmit: set submitting state and disable form controls while awaiting API response
onRegisterForChallengeSuccess: store registration result (success, registration_id, challenge_name, start_date, preferred_email_time, selected_themes, message) and trigger view change to confirmation
onRegisterForChallengeFailure: display error message (using response.message if present), clear submitting state, and keep form visible for corrections

components.functionality: Display a confirmation summary after a successful registration, including challenge_name, start_date, preferred_email_time, selected_themes, and the confirmation message. Offer navigation actions to home and to the challenges list.
data_binding: Challenge (getChallengeDetail.challenge.price_type, for navigation to challenges list)
event_handlers: onShow: only render when last registerForChallenge response.success is true or when view URL parameter indicates confirmation mode
onClickBackToChallenges: navigate to challenges.html with query parameter price_type set to getChallengeDetail.challenge.price_type

components.functionality: Show error messages when challenge_id is missing, when loading challenge details fails, or when the challenge is not available. Provide navigation options back to the list of challenges and home.
data_binding: Optional: error message from getChallengeDetail failure, if available
event_handlers: onClickBackToChallenges: navigate to challenges.html without additional filters when challenge data is not available

components.functionality: Provide contextual navigation actions, including 'Back to all challenges' (using price_type from the currently loaded challenge) and, where appropriate, 'Return to Home'. This component can be reused both in the main detail view and the confirmation view.

layout.grid_system: Visually consistent with a 12-column responsive grid centered in the viewport, with a max-width content container and substantial left/right margins.
