layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the mockup’s linear, top-to-bottom structure inside a centered max-width container. Above everything sits the site-wide utility bar and fixed header (logo left, centered nav). Immediately beneath the header, the challenge page begins with a dominant hero-style card for the challenge-summary-header sitting on the left, and a narrow vertical navigation band on the right for challenge-navigation-links. Below this hero band, content continues in a single main column: challenge description, then themes, then the registration form. Status-oriented elements (confirmation and error) appear inline within this column near the related content but do not disrupt the fundamental stacked order.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the mockup’s linear, top-to-bottom structure inside a centered max-width container. Above everything sits the site-wide utility bar and fixed header (logo left, centered nav). Immediately beneath the header, the challenge page begins with a dominant hero-style card for the challenge-summary-header sitting on the left, and a narrow vertical navigation band on the right for challenge-navigation-links. Below this hero band, content continues in a single main column: challenge description, then themes, then the registration form. Status-oriented elements (confirmation and error) appear inline within this column near the related content but do not disrupt the fundamental stacked order.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The main content container is centered and constrained to a 12-column grid with generous left/right margins. The hero (challenge-summary-header) occupies roughly 70–75% of the container width on the left and about 60–70% of the viewport height, echoing the full-bleed card in the mockup. A narrow 25–30% width vertical strip on the right is reserved for navigation controls. Below the hero, each major section (description, themes, registration) is treated as a block with 48–64px vertical spacing between sections and 24–32px internal padding. On wider screens, the textual content is limited to about 8–9 columns to maintain a deliberate white margin on the right, preserving the airy, editorial feel.
layout_strategies.content_density: The page retains a low content density. Each component is given ample breathing room, with clear separation between sections and limited on-screen text at once. Larger blocks (hero card, registration form) feel spacious inside, with generous line height, clear section headings, and restrained use of supporting text. Controls and form inputs are grouped sensibly but never tightly packed; padding around fields and buttons follows the 8–12px vertical and 16–24px horizontal guidance. The overall impression is clean and professional, never cluttered, in line with a consultancy and wellbeing brand.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow mirrors the mockup’s F-shaped pattern. The user’s eye begins at the site header (logo, navigation), then drops into the left-aligned hero card, first noticing the bold gradient and main challenge title. Within this hero, the primary CTA and key details sit toward the left and mid-to-lower area, guiding the eye downward. The thin vertical divider and navigation band on the right hint at additional content, pulling attention rightward before scrolling. As users scroll, the flow proceeds down through the description, then themes, and finally to the registration form, with confirmation and error states appearing in context near the top of the main content or above the form, remaining visually prominent yet integrated into the vertical reading path.

layout.overall_description: The Challenge Detail page sits within a centered, max-width content container beneath the site’s fixed header. At the top of the page’s main area, a large hero-style challenge card dominates the left two-thirds to three-quarters of the container, rendered with a bold blue-green gradient and plenty of white space around its text. To its right, a slim vertical band, separated by a subtle divider line, acts as the navigation/control area, echoing the carousel hint from the mockup. This top hero band occupies the majority of the initial viewport height, giving the challenge a strong, singular presence. As users scroll, the layout settles into a spacious single-column stack: a clean description block, a structured themes list, and then a substantial registration card with neatly organized fields and a clear call-to-action. Error and confirmation panels appear inline within this main column, close to the content they relate to, while preserving the generous whitespace and corporate, editorial feel. Throughout, alignment to the 12-column grid, consistent padding, and wide margins maintain a disciplined, professional visual rhythm that feels modern yet comfortably conservative.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page initializer is conceptually positioned at the very top of the main content region, directly beneath the global header and utility bar but inside the centered max-width container. It spans the full usable width of the content column (all 12 grid columns) but occupies minimal visual height, effectively appearing as an invisible, zero-height layer that prepares and governs the subsequent components. There is no distinct card or visible UI for this component; it simply anchors the start of the challenge detail layout and ensures that the hero (challenge-summary-header) always appears immediately below the header with a comfortable 48–64px top margin.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual structural component that sits at the top of the main content stack and does not affect the perceived spacing beyond ensuring consistent offset from the global header.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The challenge summary header is the dominant hero card at the top of the page’s main content. It is aligned to the left edge of the centered content container, occupying roughly 8–9 of the 12 grid columns (about 70–75% of the container width), and sits approximately 64px below the global header to create a clear visual break. Vertically, the card is tall, using around 60–70% of the initial viewport height on desktop. The card appears as a large, rounded-rectangle panel with a bold blue gradient background, generous 24–32px internal padding on all sides, and ample whitespace around the headline and key challenge details. The main challenge title and a short subtitle or strapline are positioned in the upper-left quadrant of the card, with supporting meta-information (duration, difficulty, start flexibility) arranged either in a small horizontal row or compact grid beneath. A primary “Join challenge” or equivalent CTA button is anchored toward the lower-left area of the card, ensuring it is easily discoverable without crowding the headline. The right edge of the card is visually cut short compared to the full container width, deliberately leaving space for the separate vertical navigation band to its right, with a subtle sense that the content could extend horizontally (mirroring the carousel tease in the mockup).
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static hero block that anchors the top of the main content; it scrolls naturally with the page and maintains its proportional dominance on initial load, resizing responsively while preserving its left alignment and relative width.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The challenge navigation links occupy a narrow vertical band immediately to the right of the challenge summary hero card, together forming a combined hero strip across the top of the main content. This band starts at the same vertical position as the top of the challenge-summary-header and extends down to match its approximate height on larger screens. It uses the remaining 3–4 grid columns (about 25–30% of the container width), visually separated from the hero card by a thin, full-height vertical divider line aligned along the shared boundary. Within this band, navigation controls (e.g., ‘Previous challenge’, ‘Next challenge’, ‘All challenges’) are arranged vertically with generous spacing, possibly using small pill-style links or compact cards. One rectangular control—such as a partially visible next-card teaser—is positioned towards the middle-right of the band, slightly cropped by the container edge to subtly mimic the partially visible carousel tile seen in the mockup, hinting at horizontal or sequential exploration. The rest of this band remains mostly white or very lightly tinted, ensuring it reads as secondary to the main hero card while still clearly visible in the F-pattern scan from left to right.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: A right-aligned vertical strip that scrolls with the page; on taller viewports it may behave semi-sticky within its section (remaining visible while the hero card is in view), but it does not detach from the main content container or overlap subsequent sections.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The challenge description section sits directly below the hero band (summary header plus navigation links), separated by a generous vertical gap of around 48–64px to maintain the spacious editorial rhythm. It spans the same left alignment and width as the hero card—about 8–9 columns or 70–75% of the content container—leaving a consistent white margin on the right to echo the initial layout. Visually, it appears as a clean white content block with a subtle card feel (light shadow or border) and 24–32px internal padding. A section heading such as ‘About this challenge’ sits at the top-left, followed by structured paragraphs of copy. Text lines are comfortably long but not full-width thanks to the constrained column, with generous line height and 16–24px spacing between paragraphs. There is no heavy visual framing beyond light card treatment; the emphasis is on clear, readable text with breathing room above and below the section so that it feels distinct from both the hero above and the themes list below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level section in the vertical flow; it scrolls with the page and maintains consistent width and alignment with the hero card on larger screens, collapsing to full-width on mobile while preserving top-to-bottom stacking.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The challenge themes list is placed beneath the description section, with about 40–56px of vertical spacing separating the two to keep them visually connected but clearly distinct. It is left-aligned with the description and hero card, again occupying roughly 8–9 columns of the 12-column grid, leaving the right side of the container largely white for a balanced, airy feel. The section is presented as a structured card-like block using a white or very light background, subtle border or shadow, and 24–32px internal padding. At the top-left of this block, a concise heading such as ‘Choose your theme’ or ‘Challenge focus areas’ introduces the content. Below the heading, themes are laid out as an options list that, on desktop, forms a multi-column grid (e.g., two or three columns of theme tiles), while on smaller viewports they stack vertically. Each theme option appears as a pill or small card with ample padding (8–12px vertical, 16–24px horizontal), clear labels, and possibly a short one-line descriptor. Spacing between individual theme options follows the 16–24px scale, ensuring the section remains visually calm and easy to scan, with no sense of crowding.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline block that participates in the normal vertical flow. On wider screens, it uses a responsive internal grid for theme items, but the outer block remains aligned and sized consistently with the primary content column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The challenge registration form is positioned below the themes list as the next major focal section, separated by a generous vertical gap of roughly 56–64px to signal an important interaction area. It mirrors the hero card’s left alignment and width, spanning about 8–9 columns of the 12-column grid and giving a stable, consistent column width across the main structural elements. Visually, it appears as a substantial, well-defined card with a white background, subtle shadow or border, and 24–32px padding on all sides. The form’s title (e.g., ‘Join this challenge’) sits in the top-left, with a short explanatory line beneath to reassure users. Below the heading, form fields are logically grouped: for example, preferred start date and time on the left, and any additional preferences or personal details on the right in a two-column layout on desktop. On narrower screens, these groups stack vertically. Inputs have 8–12px vertical padding inside the fields, and there is 16–24px vertical spacing between each form row, maintaining a clean, low-density feel. The primary submit button sits aligned to the bottom-right edge within the card (or centered on smaller screens) with 16–24px horizontal padding and 8–12px vertical padding, visually tying back to the hero card’s CTA styling. The overall layout encourages a clear, linear completion flow without feeling cramped.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard card that sits within the vertical content flow. It scrolls normally and retains its 8–9 column width on large screens, collapsing to full-width on smaller breakpoints while maintaining internal grouping hierarchy.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The registration confirmation panel appears in the same main content column as the registration form, using the same left alignment and width (around 8–9 columns) to maintain structural consistency. When visible, it is positioned directly above the registration form card with a moderate vertical gap (24–32px) between them, effectively pushing the form further down the page. The confirmation panel is a medium-height card with a white or very pale success-tinted background, subtle border, and 24–32px internal padding. A clear success icon or checkmark appears on the left side near the top, followed by a concise confirmation heading such as ‘You’re registered’ and a short explanatory message. Any follow-up actions (e.g., ‘Add to calendar’, ‘View your challenges’) are placed as secondary buttons or links along the bottom-right or in a small horizontal group beneath the text, with ample spacing so they don’t compete with the primary success message. The panel visually stands out due to its status color accent and proximity to the form area, but it still respects the page’s overall spaciousness and corporate aesthetic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline status card that appears conditionally within the vertical flow; it does not overlay other content but reflows the layout when shown. It scrolls normally with the page and remains anchored above the registration form until dismissed or replaced.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The challenge detail error state appears as a prominent notification band within the main content area, aligned with the central content column. For page-level or loading-related errors, it is placed directly below the hero card (and navigation band), spanning the same 8–9 column width as the hero and other sections, with about 32–40px vertical spacing separating it from the hero. For form-specific errors, it sits directly above the registration form, pushing that card downward with a similar 24–32px gap. The error display takes the form of a wide, low-profile card or banner with a white background and a strong yet tasteful red accent (border or left strip) to attract attention while still fitting the corporate look. Internal padding is around 16–24px, allowing for an error icon on the left, a concise error title and supporting text in the center-left, and an optional dismiss control or “Try again” link on the right. The text remains brief and legible, with no crowding, to keep the layout uncluttered even when presenting critical information.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditionally rendered inline banner that slots into the normal vertical flow either under the hero or above the form, depending on the error scope. It does not overlay other content or stick to the viewport; instead, it reclaims vertical space when shown and scrolls with the rest of the page.

name: Articles Listing
title: Articles
description: Searchable listing of wellbeing, performance, nutrition, and mindset articles with category filters, read-time controls, and a dedicated reading list view.

core_features: Keyword search for articles using the searchArticles interface, driven by the URL query parameter and the search form.
Category-based filtering using getArticleCategoryOptions, synchronized with the URL category parameter.
Optional read-time filtering and highlighting using the URL max_read_time parameter, passed into searchArticles filters and used to flag short reads.
Article results listing showing title, summary, category, tags, and estimated read time, with navigation to article_detail.html via article_id.
Reading list view that, when view=reading_list, uses getReadingListArticles to display articles the user has saved elsewhere on the site.
Pagination of search results using searchArticles page and page_size parameters, with current page optionally reflected in a page URL parameter.
Top-level mode switching between general article search (default) and reading list view via the URL view parameter and a UI toggle.
Cart navigation trigger from the resources section that navigates to cart.html without modifying cart contents.
user_workflows:
Search and browse articles:
1. User opens articles.html without a view URL parameter.
2. Page reads URL parameters query, category, max_read_time, and page (if present).
3. Page calls getArticleCategoryOptions to populate category filters.
4. Page constructs a filters object from category and max_read_time URL values and calls searchArticles with query, filters, page, and page_size.
5. Results are displayed in the article results list component.
6. User refines search by changing the keyword, category, or max_read_time via the UI.
7. Each change updates the corresponding URL parameter and triggers a new call to searchArticles.
8. User clicks an article card or title to navigate to article_detail.html with article_id in the URL.

Access pre-filtered articles from another page:
1. User clicks a link on index.html or another page that points to articles.html with predefined query and/or category URL parameters.
2. On load, articles.html reads the incoming query and category parameters.
3. Page calls getArticleCategoryOptions and searchArticles using those parameters.
4. The search form and filter bar display the pre-filled query and active category, and the results list shows matching articles.
5. User can adjust filters or search terms, which updates the URL and triggers new searchArticles calls.

Filter by reading time and quickly identify short reads:
1. User opens articles.html, optionally with an existing query or category.
2. User adjusts the read-time control (e.g., numeric input or slider) which updates the max_read_time URL parameter.
3. Page calls searchArticles again with filters including max_read_time.
4. Article results list highlights any articles whose estimated_read_time_minutes is less than or equal to the max_read_time value.
5. If the UI is configured to filter out longer articles, only articles satisfying the max_read_time constraint are shown.

View saved articles in the reading list:
1. From article_detail.html or another area of the site, user clicks a link or tab labeled "My Reading List" that navigates to articles.html with view=reading_list in the URL.
2. On load, articles.html detects view=reading_list and switches into reading list mode.
3. Page calls getReadingListArticles instead of searchArticles.
4. Reading list articles are displayed in a dedicated list component, including title, category, and estimated_read_time_minutes.
5. User clicks any article in the reading list to navigate to article_detail.html with article_id.
6. User can switch back to the general articles search by clicking an "All Articles" control, which navigates to articles.html without the view parameter (or with view cleared).

Paginate through search results:
1. User performs a search that returns more results than a single page_size.
2. searchArticles returns results along with total, page, and page_size.
3. Pagination controls are rendered based on these values.
4. User clicks a pagination control (e.g., next page or a specific page number), which updates the page URL parameter.
5. Page calls searchArticles again with the new page value, preserving query, category, and max_read_time from the URL.
6. The results list updates to show the selected page of articles.

Navigate to the cart from the resources section:
1. User views articles.html in either search or reading list mode.
2. User clicks the cart icon in the header or resources section.
3. Browser navigates to cart.html without altering URL parameters for articles.html.
4. If the user navigates back to articles.html, previous URL parameters still control the page state and searchArticles/getReadingListArticles behavior.
interactions: Search form submit: User enters a keyword in the search input and submits the form. The page updates the query URL parameter, resets page to the first page, and calls searchArticles with the updated query and existing filters.

Clear search input: User clears the keyword field and submits. The page removes the query URL parameter (or sets it to an empty value) and calls searchArticles without a query, leaving other filters intact.

Category filter selection: User selects a category from a category list populated by getArticleCategoryOptions. The category URL parameter is updated, page is reset to the first page, and searchArticles is called with filters including the selected category.
Category filter clearing: User deselects the category (e.g., selects a neutral option). The category URL parameter is removed or cleared, and searchArticles is called without a category constraint.

Read-time control change: User adjusts a read-time constraint (e.g., numeric field or slider). The max_read_time URL parameter is updated and searchArticles is called with filters including max_read_time. The results list updates highlight logic so articles with estimated_read_time_minutes less than or equal to max_read_time are marked as short reads.

View mode toggle to reading list: User clicks a "My Reading List" control. The page navigates to articles.html with view=reading_list in the URL, triggering a reload in reading list mode where getReadingListArticles is called.

View mode toggle to all articles: While in reading list mode, user clicks an "All Articles" control. The page navigates to articles.html without a view parameter (or with view set to a non-reading-list value), switching back to search mode where searchArticles is used.
Article card/title click: User clicks on an article in either the search results list or the reading list. Browser navigates to article_detail.html with article_id set to the clicked article's ID.

Pagination control click: User clicks next/previous or a specific page index. The page URL parameter is updated, and searchArticles is called with the new page value and the current query, category, and max_read_time values from the URL.

Active filter chip removal: User clicks a control on an active filter chip (e.g., on category or max_read_time). The corresponding URL parameter is removed or reset, and searchArticles is called with the reduced filters.

Clear all filters: User clicks a control to clear all active filters and search terms. The query, category, max_read_time, and page URL parameters are removed/reset, and searchArticles is called with no filters and an empty or default query.
Cart icon click: User clicks a cart icon in the header or resources section. Browser navigates to cart.html.

Initial load in reading list mode: When articles.html loads with view=reading_list, the page does not render the searchArticles results; instead, it calls getReadingListArticles and renders the reading list component, disabling or visually de-emphasizing search and filter controls if required.

components.functionality: Provides the functional page title, switches between 'All Articles' (search mode) and 'My Reading List' (reading list mode), and exposes a cart icon for navigation to cart.html.
Captures the user's keyword query for articles and synchronizes it with the query URL parameter used in searchArticles.
Displays selectable article categories and an input for maximum read time, then updates URL parameters category and max_read_time and triggers new article searches.

Shows currently active filters (keyword, category, max_read_time) as removable chips, keeping these in sync with the URL and triggering updated searchArticles calls when filters are removed or cleared.

Displays search results from searchArticles including title, summary, category, tags, and estimated_read_time_minutes. Uses max_read_time from the URL to mark short reads and links each article to article_detail.html with article_id.
Renders pagination based on total, page, and page_size from searchArticles and updates the page URL parameter when the user navigates between result pages.

In reading list mode (view=reading_list), fetches saved articles using getReadingListArticles and displays them as a list with title, category, and estimated_read_time_minutes, each linking to article_detail.html via article_id.

Displays contextual messages when searchArticles returns no results in search mode or getReadingListArticles returns no articles in reading list mode, providing options to adjust filters or switch modes.

Centralizes logic for determining whether the page is in search mode or reading list mode based on the view URL parameter, orchestrating calls to searchArticles or getReadingListArticles and controlling which components are rendered.

layout.grid_system: Visually consistent with a 12-column responsive grid centered in the viewport, with a max-width content container and substantial left/right margins.
layout.layout_pattern: Below the global fixed header (utility bar + main navigation), the Articles Listing page uses a centered, single main content column within a 12‑column grid. At the top of this column sits a shallow section-level header bar (articles-header-controls), followed by a larger, card-like search and filter area (search, filters, active filters) that visually behaves like the hero card from the mockup. Directly beneath this, the main results surface (articles-results-list / reading-list-view / articles-empty-state) occupies a tall, dominant card region. Along the right edge of this dominant region, a narrow vertical band houses the mode controller (articles-mode-controller), echoing the mockup’s carousel control. Pagination then sits at the bottom of the main card, still within the centered column.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Below the global fixed header (utility bar + main navigation), the Articles Listing page uses a centered, single main content column within a 12‑column grid. At the top of this column sits a shallow section-level header bar (articles-header-controls), followed by a larger, card-like search and filter area (search, filters, active filters) that visually behaves like the hero card from the mockup. Directly beneath this, the main results surface (articles-results-list / reading-list-view / articles-empty-state) occupies a tall, dominant card region. Along the right edge of this dominant region, a narrow vertical band houses the mode controller (articles-mode-controller), echoing the mockup’s carousel control. Pagination then sits at the bottom of the main card, still within the centered column.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The main content container is max-width and centered, with generous left/right margins. Inside, the section header controls span nearly the full container width but remain shallow (~56–72px tall). The hero-like search and filter block occupies roughly 70–80% of the content width (about 8–9 of 12 columns), centered, with 24–32px internal padding and 48–64px vertical spacing above and below. The results area beneath it maintains the same width, forming a continuous card that fills most of the viewport height (around 60–70%) on first load. To the right, a narrow 2–3 column vertical band is reserved for the mode controller, separated by a subtle vertical divider, leaving deliberate white space. Pagination is compact and centered, with 32–48px of breathing room above and below.
layout_strategies.content_density: The page keeps a low density, similar to the mockup: large, calm sections with clear separation. The section header and search/filters area have ample whitespace and minimal text. The article cards within the results list are spaced with 24–32px between them, avoiding visual clutter. Filters and active filters are presented in a single or double row, not stacked heavily, preserving the editorial, spacious feel appropriate for a consultancy/wellbeing brand.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The user’s eye moves from the global logo and navigation, down into the page-level header controls (section title and key toggles), then directly into the bold, central search and filter block, which acts as the hero. From there the gaze flows downward into the vertically stacked article cards. The thin vertical divider and right-hand mode controller subtly pull the eye to the right edge, hinting at an alternate Reading List mode much like the mockup’s carousel hint. Pagination at the bottom completes the F-shaped scan: top header, across controls, down through content, then a final horizontal scan across pagination.

layout.overall_description: Within a centered, max-width container below the fixed site header, the Articles Listing page presents a calm, editorial layout. A slim section header band stretches nearly full width inside the content area, introducing the articles context with room for key view toggles. Beneath it, a larger, card-like block—visually echoing the mockup’s hero—contains the search field and filter controls, sitting in the middle of the page with generous white space above, below, and to the sides. This block flows seamlessly into the main results area: a tall column of article cards aligned slightly left of center, occupying roughly two-thirds to three-quarters of the container width. Along the right edge of this main content card, a narrow vertical strip separated by a subtle divider houses a prominent mode controller that lets users switch between browsing all articles and viewing their reading list, intentionally resembling the hint of a horizontal carousel. Depending on state, the central content area shows either the article results, the reading list, or a spacious empty-state message, all sharing the same footprint. At the very bottom, centered pagination anchors the layout with generous breathing room, ensuring the overall experience feels structured, professional, and unhurried.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Positioned at the very top of the main content area, directly below the global site header, the articles-header-controls form a shallow horizontal band that spans nearly the full width of the content container (approximately 10–12 of 12 grid columns). It is aligned with the container’s left and right edges, with 24–32px of top padding separating it from the fixed header above and 24px of bottom spacing before the search block. Vertically, it is compact—roughly 56–72px tall—with the section title (e.g., “Articles”) left-aligned and any high-level view toggles or quick links (such as sort order or a compact “Reading List” count chip) aligned to the right. The background matches the page background or is only subtly differentiated, maintaining a low visual weight similar to the utility bar in the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main content flow; it scrolls normally with the page and does not overlap the fixed global navigation.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Directly beneath the header controls, the articles-search-form sits at the top of a prominent card-like block that acts as the page’s functional hero. This block is centered within the content container and spans about 8–9 of the 12 grid columns (roughly 70–80% of the container width), leaving equal white margins on the left and a slightly larger combined white space + mode-controller band on the right. The search input itself is horizontally centered within this card, occupying approximately 60–70% of the card’s width, with 16–24px padding above and below. It appears as a single, generous field with a clear, corporate styling, and at least 32px of vertical whitespace separates it from the header controls above and the filter bar below. The card’s background is white (or very light) with subtle elevation, visually anchoring this as the main interactive starting point in line with the mockup’s dominant hero tile.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, but effectively forms the top portion of a larger vertical card that continues downward into filters and the results area.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The articles-filter-bar is directly attached below the search form, within the same card-like area, maintaining the same centered width (8–9 columns). It appears as a single horizontal row of category chips, dropdowns, or segmented controls aligned to the left, with the right side of the row allowed to breathe, leaving open space or wrapping filters if necessary. There is a 16–24px gap between the bottom of the search field and the top of the filter bar, and another 8–16px gap between the filter bar and the active filters summary underneath. Overall vertical padding around this strip is comfortable, reflecting the mockup’s generous spacing. Horizontally, it maintains a clean baseline with the search form above, reinforcing a unified, hero-like control area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls with the page; optionally, on long pages, this bar can become sticky to the top of the viewport just beneath the global header to keep filtering accessible while the list scrolls.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The articles-active-filters component sits immediately below the filter bar within the same central card area, aligned to its left edge and sharing its 8–9 column width. It is a low-height strip, roughly one line of text plus chips, with 8–12px of padding above and below, and 8–16px of vertical spacing separating it from the filter bar. Active filter chips or labels are left-aligned, while the right side remains largely open, preserving the low-density feel. The visual treatment is subtle—lighter text and smaller chip sizes—so it reads as a secondary informational row before the main list begins.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main card, scrolling away with the search and filter bar as the user moves down the page (unless the filter bar is configured to stick and this strip is included with it).

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The articles-results-list occupies the dominant content region immediately below the active filters, continuing the same centered width (about 8–9 of 12 columns) to form one continuous, tall card with the search and filters above. There is a clear 24–32px gap between the active filters and the first article card. Each article item appears as an individual card or row with generous internal padding (24–32px on all sides) and 24–32px vertical spacing between items, maintaining a low-density, editorial tone. The list extends downward and fills approximately 60–70% of the viewport height on initial load, ensuring it feels like the main focus. To the right of this list, across its full height, is a narrow vertical band (about 2–3 columns wide) separated by a subtle vertical divider; this band visually pairs with the list and houses the mode controller, echoing the mockup’s right-hand carousel hint. The results list and the reading list share this footprint, with only one visible at a time.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls vertically. The list area is not independently scrollable apart from the page, providing a straightforward, linear reading experience. It shares its spatial slot with the reading-list-view and articles-empty-state, which replace it based on current mode and result state.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: articles-pagination is placed directly below the results list, still aligned with the same central width (8–9 columns) and visually anchored to the bottom of the main card. A 32–48px vertical gap separates the last article card from the pagination controls, emphasizing a clear break between content and navigation. The pagination controls themselves (page numbers, next/previous) are horizontally centered within this width, with ample horizontal padding, and do not extend into the right-hand mode-controller band. Below the pagination, another 48–64px of whitespace separates this section from any subsequent content or footer, maintaining the generous vertical rhythm seen in the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the document flow, always appearing after the current list or reading list, and scrolling off-screen normally.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The reading-list-view occupies the exact same central footprint as the articles-results-list: a tall, centered column spanning about 8–9 of 12 columns, beginning at the same vertical point where the results list would start (just below the active filters). When active (i.e., when the mode controller is switched to Reading List), it completely replaces the standard results list in this region. Its internal layout mirrors the article list’s spacing and card styles to maintain consistency, but the content is restricted to saved or favorited articles. It inherits the same right-hand relationship to the narrow vertical band and mode controller, reinforcing the sense that the controller switches the content of this main card. The vertical spacing above (from filters) and below (to pagination or an appropriate bottom margin) matches that of the results list to keep transitions between modes visually smooth.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Mutually exclusive with articles-results-list and articles-empty-state within the same spatial region. It scrolls with the page like the results list, and appears/disappears based on the state managed by the articles-mode-controller.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: articles-empty-state is displayed in the same central column that normally holds the results list or reading list, activating when no articles match the applied filters or when the reading list is empty. It is centered both horizontally within the 8–9 column width and vertically within the available viewport height where possible. The empty-state panel itself appears as a mid-sized card with a white background, subtle shadow, and 32–40px internal padding. An icon or illustration sits at the top center of this card, followed by a short heading and supporting text, with a primary action (e.g., ‘Clear filters’ or ‘Browse all articles’) beneath. There is ample whitespace surrounding this card—at least 48px above and below before reaching the filter area or pagination—to preserve the calm, spacious aesthetic and clearly signal a distinct state.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Replaces either articles-results-list or reading-list-view within the same area depending on context. Only one of these three (results list, reading list, empty state) is visible at a time. It scrolls normally if its height exceeds the viewport, but is typically compact enough to sit comfortably without requiring much scrolling.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The articles-mode-controller is positioned in a narrow vertical band immediately to the right of the main results/read-list column, mirroring the mockup’s slim carousel control area. This band spans roughly 2–3 of the 12 grid columns and runs from just above the top of the results area (approximately aligned with the bottom of the search/filter card’s header) down to at least halfway through the list area, ensuring it is visible on initial load. A subtle vertical divider separates this band from the main content card. Within this band, a vertically stacked, pill-shaped or rectangular control toggles between modes (e.g., ‘Browse’ and ‘Reading List’). The primary state (typically ‘Browse articles’) is fully visible, while the alternate state may be partially tucked or offset, mimicking the partially visible rectangular carousel control in the mockup and hinting at alternate content. The overall band remains mostly white space around the control, maintaining low visual density while still standing out as an interactive element.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Semi-sticky: as the user scrolls through the article list, the mode controller can remain pinned within its right-hand band near the top of the viewport, so that switching between modes is always accessible. It does not cover content; instead it stays within its allocated narrow column, independent of the scrolling list to its left.

name: Article Detail
title: Article
description: Displays a single article’s full content with metadata, estimated reading time, and reading-list (bookmark) controls, based on the article_id URL parameter.

core_features: Read the article_id from the URL and load full article data using getArticleDetail(articleId).
Display article title, author, publication date (from published_at), category, tags, and estimated_read_time_minutes.
Render the full article.content in a readable layout.
Show current reading-list status using is_saved_to_reading_list from getArticleDetail.
Allow the user to add the article to their reading list with a single click using saveArticleToReadingList(articleId).
Update the UI to indicate the saved/bookmarked state after a successful save (e.g., button label/state change).
Provide a link to navigate back to the articles listing (articles.html) optionally filtered by the article’s category via URL parameters.
Allow navigation to the articles listing filtered by category when a user clicks the category or a tag (articles.html?category=...).
After saving, provide a link to open the articles listing in 'reading list' view (articles.html?view=reading_list).
Handle loading and error states for the article fetch (e.g., show loading indicator until getArticleDetail resolves; show an error message if it fails or article_id is invalid).
user_workflows:
Workflow: Open an article from the listing
1) User clicks an article card on articles.html, which navigates to article_detail.html?article_id=<ARTICLE_ID>.
2) Article Detail page reads the article_id URL parameter.
3) The page calls getArticleDetail(articleId) with the parsed article_id.
4) While waiting, a loading indicator is shown and no article content is displayed yet.
5) On success, the page displays the article title, metadata, estimated reading time, and full content.
6) The is_saved_to_reading_list flag from the response is used to set the initial state of the reading-list control (e.g., 'Saved' vs 'Save to Reading List').

Workflow: Add an article to the reading list
1) User is viewing article_detail.html with a loaded article.
2) The reading-list control shows a state based on is_saved_to_reading_list from getArticleDetail.
3) If the article is not yet saved, the control is displayed as an actionable button (e.g., 'Add to Reading List').
4) User clicks the button.
5) The page calls saveArticleToReadingList(articleId) with the current article.id.
6) While waiting for the response, the button can show a temporary loading state to prevent double submissions.
7) On success (success = true), the UI updates the saved state (e.g., button label changes to 'Saved to Reading List').
8) After a successful save, a 'View Reading List' navigation link/button is shown or enabled, which leads to articles.html?view=reading_list when clicked.
9) If saveArticleToReadingList returns success = false, an error message is displayed and the saved state remains unchanged.

Workflow: Navigate back to articles listing
1) User is viewing article_detail.html for a specific article.
2) User clicks a 'Back to Articles' control.
3) The page navigates to articles.html.
4) Optionally, if the article has a category, the navigation can include a category URL parameter, e.g., articles.html?category=<ARTICLE_CATEGORY>, so the listing page can focus on that category.

Workflow: Explore more articles via category or tags
1) While reading the article, user sees the article category and tags displayed.
2) User clicks on the category or one of the tags.
3) The page navigates to articles.html with a category URL parameter derived from the clicked category/tag (e.g., articles.html?category=<CATEGORY_OR_TAG_VALUE>), enabling the listing page to show related articles based on that value.
4) The Article Detail page itself does not fetch related articles directly; it only provides navigation via URL parameters to the listings page.

Workflow: View Reading List from an article
1) User saves the article using the reading-list control (see previous workflow).
2) After a successful save, a 'View Reading List' control becomes visible or remains visible.
3) User clicks 'View Reading List'.
4) The page navigates to articles.html?view=reading_list, allowing the listing page to show the user’s reading list view.
interactions: On page load: Read article_id from the URL query string; if present, call getArticleDetail(articleId); show loading indicator until the response is received.

On successful getArticleDetail response: Bind article fields (title, summary, content, category, tags, estimated_read_time_minutes, published_at, author_name) to the display components; bind is_saved_to_reading_list to the reading-list toggle state.

On getArticleDetail failure or missing article_id: Show an error state component indicating the article cannot be loaded and provide a link back to articles.html (without calling any additional interfaces).

On click 'Add to Reading List' (when is_saved_to_reading_list is false): Call saveArticleToReadingList(articleId); disable the button or show a loading state while waiting; on success, update the saved state to true; on failure, show an error message and keep state as false.

On click 'Saved' (when is_saved_to_reading_list is true): The button is non-destructive for this page (no removal from reading list on this page, as there is no corresponding interface); clicking can be treated as a no-op or can show a short message indicating it is already saved.
On click 'Back to Articles': Navigate to articles.html, optionally including ?category=<article.category> if available, using standard link navigation.
On click article category label: Navigate to articles.html?category=<article.category> so the listing page can filter articles.

On click tag chip: Navigate to articles.html?category=<clicked_tag> (or another appropriate query parameter name consistent with the articles page), mapping the clicked tag to the URL parameter value.
On click 'View Reading List': Navigate to articles.html?view=reading_list to allow the listing page to display the reading list view.
On content render: No additional interface calls; simply output article.content and ensure it updates if a new article_id is loaded via URL change.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The primary state driver is the article_id URL parameter. When article_detail.html is loaded, the page parses article_id from the query string. If article_id is present, getArticleDetail(articleId) is called once; the response defines the page's main state: article data (Article model) and is_saved_to_reading_list flag (ReadingList model state). No localStorage or client-side persistence is used. If article_id changes (e.g., via internal navigation that updates the URL), the page should treat it as a fresh load: clear current article state, show loading, and call getArticleDetail again.

Navigation out of this page uses URL parameters to communicate intent to articles.html. Clicking 'Back to Articles' or a category/tag sets articles.html?category=<derived_value>, where <derived_value> comes from article.category or the clicked tag string. Clicking 'View Reading List' sets articles.html?view=reading_list. The Article Detail page does not interpret these outgoing parameters; it only constructs them. Reading-list membership is not directly mutated except via saveArticleToReadingList; removal or further management happens on other pages that use different interfaces. The saved-state UI on this page is fully derived from is_saved_to_reading_list returned by getArticleDetail plus any in-session update following a successful saveArticleToReadingList call.

components.functionality: Initializes the page by reading article_id from the URL query string, calling getArticleDetail(articleId), and managing loading/error states as well as propagating the loaded article data and is_saved_to_reading_list flag to child components.
Displays the article title and metadata including author_name, published_at, category, tags, and estimated_read_time_minutes. Provides clickable elements for navigating via category or tags.
Renders the full article.content for reading, using the currently loaded Article data from the loader component. Updates automatically when a new article is loaded by article-detail-loader.

Shows an action button reflecting whether the article is already in the reading list. If is_saved_to_reading_list is false, it shows an actionable state (e.g., 'Add to Reading List') and, when clicked, calls saveArticleToReadingList(article.id). On success, it updates the state to reflect that the article is now saved and can display a confirmation message. If already saved, it shows a non-destructive 'Saved' state.

Provides a 'View Reading List' navigation control to open the articles listing in reading-list view (articles.html?view=reading_list). It should be visible or emphasized after the article is successfully saved to the reading list, or whenever the current article is already marked as saved.

Displays general navigation controls for this page, such as 'Back to Articles'. It can use the current article’s category to build a more specific navigation URL (articles.html?category=<article.category>) when available, otherwise it can navigate to articles.html without extra parameters.
Shows a loading state while getArticleDetail is in progress and hides itself when the article data has been loaded or an error occurs.

Displays an error message when the article cannot be loaded (e.g., missing article_id or getArticleDetail error) and offers a link back to the articles listing (articles.html). It does not call any interfaces.

layout.grid_system: Visually consistent with a 12-column responsive grid centered in the viewport, with a max-width content container and substantial left/right margins.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the mockup’s top-to-bottom structure: global utility bar at the very top, then the fixed main header with logo and centered navigation. Below this, a centered main content column houses a dominant, card-like article header (hero) that visually echoes the bold blue gradient tile from the mockup. To the right of this header, a narrow vertical band is reserved for the reading-list controls, mimicking the mockup’s carousel hint. The full article body is stacked directly beneath the hero card in a single-column editorial flow, followed by a horizontal article navigation bar at the bottom of the content column. Status components (loader, loading indicator, error state) temporarily occupy or overlay the same central content region rather than introducing new structural areas.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the mockup’s top-to-bottom structure: global utility bar at the very top, then the fixed main header with logo and centered navigation. Below this, a centered main content column houses a dominant, card-like article header (hero) that visually echoes the bold blue gradient tile from the mockup. To the right of this header, a narrow vertical band is reserved for the reading-list controls, mimicking the mockup’s carousel hint. The full article body is stacked directly beneath the hero card in a single-column editorial flow, followed by a horizontal article navigation bar at the bottom of the content column. Status components (loader, loading indicator, error state) temporarily occupy or overlay the same central content region rather than introducing new structural areas.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The utility bar is shallow and full-width; the main header band is also full-width with generous vertical padding. The main content container is constrained to a max width and centered, using a 12-column grid. Within this container, the article header card spans roughly 8–9 of 12 columns (about 70% of container width) anchored to the left, while the rightmost 3–4 columns remain mostly white space, interrupted by a vertical divider and the reading-list controls. Vertically, the hero/metadata card occupies about 60–70% of the initial viewport height with generous white space above and below it. The article body below uses the same horizontal alignment and width as the hero, with comfortable vertical spacing between sections. The article navigation bar uses a slim horizontal band beneath the body, spanning the same width as the article content.
layout_strategies.content_density: Density stays low and editorial, emphasizing comfort for reading. The hero card has a large gradient background, a clear title, and a minimal metadata row, with no clutter around it. Line lengths in the article body are kept to a readable measure (roughly 60–75 characters) with ample line-height and clear separation between paragraphs, headings, and lists. Controls (reading-list toggle and CTA, article navigation bar) are visually compact but surrounded by generous padding and whitespace so they feel deliberate rather than noisy. Status states (loader, error) present a small amount of focused content in the same generous card format.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual journey mirrors the mockup’s F-shaped pattern. Users first notice the top-left logo in the fixed header, then scan right across the centered primary navigation. Their gaze is then drawn down to the prominent blue gradient article header card in the main content area. Within that card, the article title and the bright gradient become the focal point, followed by a left-to-right scan of metadata (date, category, reading time). The eye then shifts slightly right toward the vertical divider and the reading-list controls in the narrow right-hand band, which subtly signal additional actions. As users scroll, the flow becomes strictly vertical: continuing down through the body text, then landing on the article navigation bar at the bottom, which invites the next action (next/previous article or back to listing).

layout.overall_description: The Article Detail page sits within a clean, corporate shell: a full-width utility bar at the very top, followed by a fixed header band with the logo on the left and main navigation centered horizontally. Beneath this, the main content lives inside a centered, max-width 12-column grid. At first view, the dominant element is a large, card-like article header on a bold blue gradient that occupies roughly two-thirds to three-quarters of the content width and about two-thirds of the viewport height. It is left-aligned, leaving a deliberate white column on the right where a subtle vertical divider and vertically stacked reading-list controls live, referencing the mockup’s carousel handle. Below this hero, the article content flows in a comfortable single column, using the same left alignment and width, with generous vertical spacing between sections to maintain a light, editorial feel. At the bottom of the article content, a slim, horizontally oriented article navigation bar anchors the layout, offering next/previous article and possibly a link back to the article listing. When loading or error states occur, they occupy this same central hero/body region with card-like treatments, preserving the clean, spacious hierarchy of the original mockup.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article-detail-loader governs the main content region directly beneath the fixed site header. When active (initial data fetch), it occupies the entire central content column where the hero card and article body would normally appear. Visually, it should mimic the proportions of the article layout: a wide placeholder card centered within the max-width container, spanning about 70% of the container width and aligned to the left, with a tall height approximating 60–70% of the viewport. Skeleton lines or blocks within this area suggest the article title near the top-left, a short row for metadata below it, and several full-width text lines extending downward to represent the article body. The rightmost narrow column of the grid (about the remaining 30% of width) remains mostly white, with an optional thin vertical placeholder line to hint at the future reading-list controls, preserving the overall composition even while loading.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: When active, it temporarily replaces the article-header-metadata and article-content-body in the main content area, but does not affect the fixed global header. It scrolls with the page like normal content. Once data has loaded, it disappears entirely, freeing the space for the actual article components.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article-header-metadata component is presented as a large, hero-style card positioned immediately below the fixed site header, centered in the viewport but aligned to the left within the max-width container. The card spans approximately 8–9 of the 12 grid columns (about 70% of the container width), with its left edge aligned to the content column’s left margin, and its right edge stopping well before the container’s right margin, leaving a distinct white vertical strip on the right. Vertically, the card takes up roughly two-thirds of the visible viewport height on load, with generous top margin from the header (around 48–64px) and similar margin before the article body begins. Inside the card, a bold blue-to-teal gradient forms the background. The article title is set in large type and sits in the upper-left quadrant with clear breathing room: about 24–32px padding from the top and left edges. Directly beneath the title, a single horizontal row contains metadata (category, publish date, estimated reading time), laid out left-to-right with small separators, all anchored to the left. Additional subtitle or short synopsis text, if present, sits below the metadata with generous line-height. The overall effect is a clean, editorial hero that dominates the central view, with clear left alignment and an intentional visual gap on the right for interactive controls.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a standard block element within the main scrollable content. On smaller screens, it becomes a full-width card (spanning nearly 100% of the container), still maintaining generous padding and vertical spacing, but the narrow right column collapses beneath it.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article-content-body sits directly below the article-header-metadata card, aligned to the same left margin within the main content container, so the left edges of the hero card and the body text form a clean vertical line. Its width matches or very slightly exceeds the hero card’s width, spanning roughly 8–9 grid columns (around 70% of the container width) to create an optimal reading line length. There is a generous vertical gap (about 48px) between the bottom of the hero card and the top of the first line of article text. Within the content body, text flows in a single column with comfortable line-height and 16–24px vertical spacing between paragraphs. Headings introduce sub-sections with 24–32px of top margin, while lists and pull quotes are indented minimally but kept within the same column. The right-hand narrow column established by the hero’s layout continues visually as mostly white space alongside the article body, ensuring the central reading column remains visually anchored and uncluttered. On long articles, this creates a tall, vertically scrolling reading experience that remains calm and spacious.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard scrollable content that extends vertically well beyond the initial viewport as needed. On smaller screens, it reflows to use nearly full container width, while preserving generous horizontal padding for readability.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The reading-list-toggle appears as a compact icon button or pill-shaped control associated with the article header, placed in the narrow right-hand band that parallels the hero card. Horizontally, it sits to the immediate right of the hero card’s edge, within the remaining 3–4 grid columns, visually separated from the card by a thin vertical divider that runs roughly the height of the hero card’s central area. The toggle aligns vertically with the top third of the hero card: its center is roughly level with the upper portion of the article title. There is ample white space around it—about 16–24px padding from the divider and from the container’s right edge—so it feels like a deliberate, floating control rather than a crowded button. The placement makes it easy to spot without competing with the title, echoing the mockup’s partially visible carousel control in the right-hand gutter. On hover or focus, subtle elevation or border changes reinforce its interactivity, but the overall style remains minimal and corporate.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: On desktop, it remains visually attached to the hero region; as the user scrolls, it scrolls away with the hero. On long articles, an optional enhancement is to make a smaller version of this toggle sticky within the right margin once the user scrolls past the hero, pinning it to the top-right of the main content viewport (within the container’s right boundary) to keep the reading-list action accessible without disrupting the layout. On tablet/mobile, it drops beneath the hero card’s metadata row, left-aligned above the article body for a simpler stacked layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The reading-list-cta is presented as a more descriptive navigation control (e.g., a pill-shaped or small rectangular button saying “View Reading List” or similar) that visually reinforces the mockup’s partially visible carousel tile. It is positioned directly below the reading-list-toggle in the same right-hand vertical band. Horizontally, it sits just to the right of the vertical divider, and its right edge may extend slightly beyond the main hero card’s vertical alignment, giving the subtle impression that it is a card sliding in from off-canvas. The control is oriented horizontally, about the width of 2–3 grid columns, but appears as a compact block with 12–16px vertical padding and 24–32px horizontal padding. Vertically, it is placed in the middle third of the hero card’s height, leaving clear whitespace above (around 16–24px from the toggle) and below. This mirrors the mockup’s sense that there are more stories or actions reachable via a lateral movement, though in this context it links to the user’s reading list. Below the hero, the right-hand band visually tapers off, and the CTA’s prominence diminishes as the main body takes over.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: It behaves as a fixed-position element relative to the hero area: it scrolls with the page and disappears as the user scrolls past the hero card. On smaller screens, it stacks below the reading-list-toggle beneath the hero metadata, left-aligned, creating a vertical cluster of article-related actions just before the article body.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article-navigation-bar appears as a slim, horizontal control band at the bottom of the article-content-body, visually acting as a footer for the article detail. It spans the same width as the article body—about 8–9 grid columns—and aligns perfectly with the body’s left edge so the entire article column feels cohesive. A subtle top border or change in background tone differentiates it from the body text above without breaking the clean, corporate aesthetic. Inside the bar, navigation elements such as “Previous article,” “Next article,” and possibly a link back to the article listing are arranged left-to-right, with the primary next/previous controls given more weight (e.g., bold text or subtle arrows). Padding inside the bar mirrors the spacing system: around 16px vertical and 24px horizontal internal padding. There is generous whitespace above it (about 32–48px from the final paragraph), ensuring it feels like a distinct navigational unit rather than the continuation of the article text.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: By default, it behaves as a standard block at the end of the article. As an optional enhancement consistent with modern patterns, it can become a low-profile, sticky bar once the user scrolls past it—pinning to the bottom edge of the viewport within the main content width—providing persistent next/previous controls without obscuring content. On smaller screens, it spans the full container width and stacks the navigation items vertically or centers them for easier tapping.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loading-indicator is a focused status element that appears within the main content column when data for the article is being fetched or refreshed. It is centered both horizontally and vertically within the space that the article-header-metadata would occupy. In the simplest form, this could be a spinner or progress ring surrounded by ample white space, placed approximately in the center of the hero card’s expected area. The indicator sits within the max-width container, not full-bleed, maintaining the same left and right margins as the article content. If paired with a short status message (e.g., “Loading article…”), the text appears directly below the spinner with about 8–12px vertical separation, all contained within an invisible or very subtle card footprint that respects the layout’s generous spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: It overlays or replaces the inner content of the article-detail-loader region, but remains within the flow of the document rather than fixed to the viewport. Once loading completes, it is removed from the DOM and yields its space back to the hero and body content. In error states, it does not appear (the error-state-display takes precedence).

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The error-state-display occupies the same central position as the article header and body when an article cannot be loaded. It appears as a card-like block centered in the main content container beneath the fixed site header, with a width similar to the hero card—around 8–9 grid columns—and generous padding (24–32px all around). Vertically, the card is given enough height and surrounding whitespace to feel calm and deliberate, roughly 40–50% of the viewport height, with ample space above and below. Inside, an error icon or subtle graphic sits near the top-left or centered horizontally, followed by a concise error message and optional explanatory text. Any retry or navigation actions (e.g., “Try again,” “Back to articles”) are placed below the message, aligned left and spaced using the same button spacing conventions defined in the design DNA. The right-hand narrow band that normally contains reading-list controls is left empty or shows only the faint vertical divider to avoid cluttering the error state, keeping attention on the message.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: It fully replaces the article-header-metadata and article-content-body in the layout when an error occurs, occupying their combined space within the central column. It scrolls with the page (though it will usually fit within one viewport). Once the error is resolved or the user navigates away, it disappears, returning the layout to its standard article presentation.

name: Memberships & Add-ons
title: Memberships & Add-ons
description: Search and select gym membership plans and yoga add-ons/class passes, apply filters such as billing period and benefits, add selected options to the cart, and review the combined recurring cost before checkout.

core_features: Load dynamic membership filter options (billing periods and benefits) using getMembershipFilterOptions and render them as selectable filters.
Search membership plans with searchMembershipPlans based on current filters (including billing_period, selected benefits, and an optional maximum price threshold), and display paginated results.
Highlight or filter membership plans that include gym access, using benefit filters and the includes_gym_access flag returned from searchMembershipPlans.
Allow users to select a membership plan, view detailed information via getMembershipPlanDetail, choose a start date, and add the membership as a recurring item to the cart using addMembershipToCart.
Load yoga add-on filter options (billing periods and minimum classes per week) using getYogaAddOnFilterOptions and render them in the yoga add-ons filter section.
Search yoga add-ons/class passes using searchYogaAddOns based on filters such as billing_period and minimum classes_per_week, and display paginated results.
Allow users to select yoga add-ons, choose a start date, and add them as recurring items to the cart using addAddOnToCart.
Retrieve and display a cart summary using getCartSummary, showing all items added from this page plus any existing items, including recurring subtotals.

Compute and display an indicative combined monthly cost for selected memberships and yoga add-ons by aggregating relevant recurring items (e.g., item_type 'membership' and 'add_on' with monthly billing_period) from getCartSummary.
Provide an optional user-defined monthly budget input and compare the combined recurring monthly total to this budget, visually indicating whether the user is within or over their target budget.
Support navigation to cart.html (for full review/checkout) and back to index.html, while preserving current filter and budget state in URL parameters.
user_workflows:
1) Page load with optional preselected filters:
- User navigates to memberships.html, optionally with URL parameters billing_period and benefit (e.g., from index.html).
- The page-state-manager component reads billing_period and benefit from the URL.
- The page triggers getMembershipFilterOptions and getYogaAddOnFilterOptions in parallel.
- Once membership filters load, the membership-filter-bar initializes its controls:
  - billing_period defaults to the billing_period URL value when present; otherwise, it may default to a commonly used period present in the returned options (for example, the first billing period in the list or a period matching a business rule such as monthly if available).
  - benefit defaults to the benefit URL value when present; otherwise, no benefit is preselected.
  - Any additional URL parameters such as membership_price_cap, membership_page, yoga_min_classes, yoga_billing_period, yoga_page, and budget_monthly are also read and applied.
- The page then calls searchMembershipPlans with the constructed filters and page information, and calls searchYogaAddOns with yoga-specific filters.
- In parallel, getCartSummary is called to load existing cart items and populate the cart-summary-panel and combined-monthly-budget-panel.

2) Filter memberships and choose a gym access plan under a chosen price threshold:
- In the membership-filter-bar, the user selects a billing period (for example, a recurring period like monthly) from the dropdown.
- The user optionally selects one or more benefits (e.g., gym_access) using the benefit selector or a dedicated 'Gym access only' toggle that sets the benefit filter accordingly.
- The user enters a numeric maximum membership price into the 'Max membership price' field. This value becomes the price cap filter.
- On filter change or when the user clicks an 'Apply filters' control, membership-filter-bar updates the URL query parameters (billing_period, benefit, membership_price_cap, membership_page) without reloading the page and triggers a call to searchMembershipPlans with the current filters.
- membership-list updates with the new results. Plans that meet the filters (including includes_gym_access where relevant and price less than or equal to the user’s maximum price) are shown.
- The user reviews the list and clicks 'View details' on a chosen plan to open membership-detail-modal, which calls getMembershipPlanDetail with the selected membership_plan_id.
- In membership-detail-modal, the user selects a start date for the membership and submits the form.
- The modal calls addMembershipToCart with the selected membershipPlanId and start_date. On success, it triggers getCartSummary to refresh the cart summary and budget panel and visually confirms that the membership was added.

3) Select yoga add-ons that provide at least a chosen number of classes per week:
- In the yoga-addon-filter-bar, the user selects a billing period (e.g., aligned with the membership billing period when appropriate) from the dropdown populated via getYogaAddOnFilterOptions.
- The user chooses a minimum classes_per_week value from options provided by getYogaAddOnFilterOptions (for example, selecting a value representing at least one class per week, using the available numeric options).
- Optionally, the user can enter a maximum price for yoga add-ons.
- On filter change or on clicking 'Apply filters', yoga-addon-filter-bar updates URL parameters (yoga_billing_period, yoga_min_classes, yoga_page, and any yoga price-related parameter) and calls searchYogaAddOns with these filters.
- yoga-addon-list displays the updated results, showing add-on name, billing_period, classes_per_week, and price.
- The user clicks 'Add to cart' on a chosen yoga add-on. A simple inline form or modal appears to select a start date.
- The component calls addAddOnToCart with the chosen addOnId and start_date. On success, it calls getCartSummary to update cart-summary-panel and combined-monthly-budget-panel.

4) Review combined monthly cost vs. user budget:
- At any time, the user can view cart-summary-panel, which shows current cart items retrieved via getCartSummary, and combined-monthly-budget-panel, which calculates indicative recurring totals.
- The user enters a numeric value into a 'Monthly budget' field in combined-monthly-budget-panel.
- The panel uses getCartSummary data to identify recurring items with a monthly billing_period and item_type of 'membership' or 'add_on'.
- It sums the unit_price × quantity or subtotal_price of these items to compute a combined monthly total for memberships and add-ons.
- The panel compares this total against the user’s budget, providing clear indication if the current combination is within or above the budget.
- The budget value is written to the URL as budget_monthly so that reloading or sharing the link preserves this budget setting.
- If the user changes membership or add-on selections (adding or removing items from the cart from this page or elsewhere in the site), a subsequent call to getCartSummary updates the totals and budget comparison.

5) Navigate to full cart review or back to home:
- After confirming their selections, the user clicks a 'View Cart' or 'Review Subscription' button in the cart-summary-panel.
- The page navigates to cart.html without additional required parameters (the cart is resolved server-side), while preserving any non-critical URL state only if desired (e.g., not strictly required for cart.html).
- Alternatively, the user can click 'Back to Home' or the logo in the header navigation, triggering a navigation to index.html. The memberships.html state encoded in URL parameters is naturally preserved in the browser history for back/forward navigation.
interactions:
Change membership billing period:
- Interaction: User selects a new billing period option in membership-filter-bar.
- Effect: membership-filter-bar updates internal filter state, writes billing_period to the URL query, resets membership_page to the first page, and triggers searchMembershipPlans with the updated billing_period and other current filters. membership-list updates with the API response.

Toggle gym access benefit:
- Interaction: User checks or unchecks a 'Gym access only' option or selects a benefit that corresponds to gym access in a benefit dropdown.
- Effect: membership-filter-bar updates the selected benefit(s) in its state, writes benefit to the URL query, and calls searchMembershipPlans with the updated benefits filter. Results in membership-list reflect only plans that include the selected benefit when the filter is active.

Set maximum membership price (price threshold):
- Interaction: User enters or adjusts a numeric 'Max membership price' field and confirms via blur, Enter, or an 'Apply filters' control.
- Effect: membership-filter-bar updates its price cap state, writes membership_price_cap to the URL query, and triggers searchMembershipPlans with an additional price_cap filter. Only memberships with price less than or equal to this cap are included in membership-list.

Paginate membership results:
- Interaction: User clicks 'Next', 'Previous', or a specific page number in membership-list pagination controls.
- Effect: membership-list updates membership_page in URL, calls searchMembershipPlans with the same filters but with the new page value, and re-renders results. The page number in the UI synchronizes with the returned page/page_size/total.

View membership details:
- Interaction: User clicks 'View details' on a membership card/row in membership-list.
- Effect: membership-detail-modal is shown and calls getMembershipPlanDetail with the selected membershipPlanId. The modal binds to the returned membership object to display rich details and price information.

Add membership to cart:
- Interaction: In membership-detail-modal (or an inline add form), user selects a start date and clicks 'Add to cart'.
- Effect: The component calls addMembershipToCart with membershipPlanId and start_date. On success (success === true), it shows a confirmation, closes the modal if applicable, triggers getCartSummary to refresh cart-summary-panel and combined-monthly-budget-panel, and may visually mark the membership as 'Added' in membership-list. On failure, it displays the message returned by addMembershipToCart without updating the cart summary.

Change yoga add-on filters:
- Interaction: User selects a billing period and minimum classes per week in yoga-addon-filter-bar and optionally adjusts a maximum price field.
- Effect: yoga-addon-filter-bar updates its internal yoga filter state, writes yoga_billing_period and yoga_min_classes (and any price-related parameter) to URL, resets yoga_page to the first page, and calls searchYogaAddOns. yoga-addon-list renders the updated results.

Paginate yoga add-on results:
- Interaction: User clicks pagination controls in yoga-addon-list.
- Effect: yoga-addon-list updates yoga_page in URL, calls searchYogaAddOns with the same filters but the new page value, and updates the displayed add-ons.

Add yoga add-on to cart:
