A clear section heading sits at the top of this block, followed by horizontally arranged payment options (e.g., cards, wallets) that appear as pill or card-like buttons in a row on wide screens. These option cards are arranged left-to-right with consistent 16–24px gaps; if space is constrained, they wrap gracefully onto a second line while maintaining equal spacing. When a payment method is selected, its corresponding fields appear directly beneath the options in a vertically stacked sub-block, still within the visual boundary of this section. The section is visually separated from neighboring form sections by 24–32px of vertical whitespace, preserving the low-density, editorial feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Anchored within the checkout form’s flow; it does not float independently. Its internal layout is responsive: horizontal pill/card arrangement on desktop, stacking or wrapping on smaller screens while maintaining alignment with the form’s grid.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The terms and policies consent area is positioned near the bottom of the checkout form card, immediately above the primary submission control (within the form) and any inline form-level messages. It spans the usable text width of the form, aligned to the left with the rest of the content. The checkbox is placed at the left margin of the text column, with the label and linked policy text extending to the right in one or two lines, depending on screen width. There is a comfortable 16–24px vertical gap above this component (separating it from the payment fields) and another 16–24px gap below it before the primary button, ensuring it feels distinct and noticeable. The overall block is compact but given enough surrounding whitespace that the consent does not feel like a minor footnote, aligning with the professional, transparent tone of the site.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and anchored at the bottom of the checkout form content. On smaller screens, the text wraps within the existing form width, but the checkbox remains left-aligned, preserving clarity.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The submission state banner sits directly below the main site header and above the hero-like checkout content, spanning the full width of the central content container. It is a shallow horizontal bar (approximately 48–64px in height) with a subtle yet noticeable background color (e.g., soft green for success, amber or red for errors), visually distinct but not overpowering. The message is centered vertically and aligned slightly left-of-center horizontally, respecting the same left margin used by the main checkout card. The banner has 16–24px of padding above and below the text, and enough left/right padding so the text doesn’t feel cramped. When present, it pushes the main checkout card and right summary band downward uniformly, preserving the vertical rhythm. When absent, the main cards shift up, occupying the space for a seamless impression. The banner feels like a slim, editorial strip rather than a large modal, in keeping with the clean, professional aesthetic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and inline within the top of the main content area, directly beneath the fixed site header. Appears conditionally (e.g., after a failed submission or to show progress) and scrolls out of view with the rest of the page content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Upon successful completion of the checkout, the order confirmation panel replaces the checkout form in the primary hero area on the left, occupying the same footprint as the former form card. It appears as a large, well-padded card (24–32px padding) spanning roughly 7–8 grid columns, aligned to the left within the content container and sharing the same vertical alignment and bottom spacing as the previous form. The card’s composition follows a top-down, editorial structure: a prominent confirmation headline near the top (e.g., “Order confirmed”), a visual confirmation cue (such as an abstract tick mark or icon) placed near the left upper quadrant, then brief explanatory text and key confirmation details (order number, date, concise summary) stacked vertically with clear 16–24px spacing. Below that, secondary information such as what happens next or links to view the full order may be presented. The right-hand summary band remains in place, but the confirmation card likely references the same totals, reinforcing the relationship between the left and right content. The confirmation card, like the original hero card in the mockup, conveys clarity and completion with ample whitespace and minimal clutter.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditionally replaces the checkout-form component in the main left column once submission succeeds. It is static within the layout and scrolls naturally with the page; on mobile, it occupies full width and stacks above the summary content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component operates behind the scenes to manage the submission process and does not render any direct UI. Conceptually, it is associated with the lower portion of the checkout form card, orchestrating the state of the primary submit action and interaction with the submission state banner and confirmation panel. While visually invisible, its presence is felt through button loading states and the transition from form to confirmation.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual controller; it affects state and interactions but takes up no visual space. It is logically tied to the main checkout form area and the submission-state-banner/order-confirmation-panel transitions.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The view mode state manager is an invisible orchestrator that determines whether the user sees the empty-cart notice, the active checkout form, or the order confirmation panel. It does not have a visual box of its own but defines which major hero-like component occupies the primary left-hand card space at any given time. In practice, it controls the dominant content in the main hero region, preserving consistent spacing and alignment as views change.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual state manager that swaps which primary content card is rendered in the main content area. No direct spatial footprint, but it ensures smooth layout transitions without shifting the underlying grid or margins.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The checkout navigation bar is a low-profile horizontal control band positioned near the bottom of the main content area, aligned with the central content container. On larger screens, it sits below the main left-right card group with a vertical gap of about 48–64px, giving the primary cards breathing room. The bar spans roughly the same width as the main container (up to its max width) but is visually lighter than the header, with a subtle background (e.g., a very light gray or faint gradient) and a top border or shadow that separates it from the content above. Within the bar, secondary navigation like “Back to cart” is aligned to the left, while the primary action (e.g., “Place order” when the form is active) and possibly a small total summary are aligned to the right, echoing the balanced, corporate aesthetic. Padding of 16–24px vertically ensures touch-friendly controls without making the bar feel bulky. On shorter viewports, the bar may appear pinned near the bottom of the viewport, maintaining visibility of primary actions as the user scrolls through the form, while still respecting the general spaciousness by not overlapping core content aggressively.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a static band beneath the main content on tall viewports; can become sticky to the bottom of the viewport on desktop and mobile when vertical space is constrained, ensuring that primary navigation actions remain accessible while scrolling. It remains constrained within the max-width container horizontally.

name: About & Info
title: About & Info
description: Page for viewing background information about the sports consultancy, its approach and team, and for accessing contact, FAQ, booking, and legal information, including a contact form.

core_features: Load and display mission and philosophy text for the consultancy using getAboutPageContent.
Show a brief list of key coaches/practitioners with their role and short bio from getAboutPageContent.coaches.
Display contact details (email and phone) from getAboutPageContent and present a contact form that submits inquiries via submitContactInquiry.
Provide an FAQs summary section using getAboutPageContent.faqs_summary, with links or anchors to more detailed FAQs if available elsewhere.

Provide informational sections for terms of service, privacy information, and how bookings, cancellations, and programs are handled (content supplied outside of this page’s interfaces, but structured and navigable here).
Support direct navigation to specific sections (contact, faqs, terms, privacy, etc.) via the URL section parameter and internal anchor links.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1 – View general information about the consultancy:
1. User opens about.html with no section parameter.
2. about-page-controller calls getAboutPageContent once on initialization.
3. While data is loading, mission, philosophy, coach list, contact, and FAQs areas show a loading state.
4. Once getAboutPageContent resolves, mission-section and philosophy-section render mission and philosophy text.
5. coaches-summary-list renders the coach list from the returned coaches array (name, role, short bio).
6. User can scroll the page or click internal navigation links to jump between mission, philosophy, coaches, FAQs, bookings info, terms, privacy, and contact sections.

Workflow 2 – Deep link to a specific information section (e.g., contact):
1. User clicks an About/Contact/FAQ/Terms/Privacy link in the footer from any page; the link points to about.html with a section URL parameter (for example, about.html?section=contact).
2. about-page-controller parses the section parameter from the URL and sets currentSection accordingly.
3. The controller triggers getAboutPageContent to fetch shared page content.
4. After data is loaded, section-anchor-navigation scrolls the viewport to the component whose section key matches the section parameter (e.g., contact-section).
5. The matching section may be visually highlighted (via component state) based on currentSection (for example, contact tab/link marked active).

Workflow 3 – Submit a contact inquiry:
1. User navigates (by scrolling or clicking a nav link) to the contact-section.
2. contact-info-block displays contact_email and contact_phone from getAboutPageContent for quick reference.
3. User fills out contact-form fields: name (optional), email (required), phone (optional), subject (optional), message (required).
4. On form submission, contact-form performs client-side validation to ensure email and message are present and syntactically valid.
5. If validation fails, form-status-indicator shows error messages and no API call is made.
6. If validation passes, contact-form calls submitContactInquiry with the current field values and disables the submit button while waiting for a response.
7. When submitContactInquiry returns, form-status-indicator displays success or error based on the success flag; on success, the form may clear its fields; on failure, fields remain so the user can retry.
8. The response’s inquiry_id, if provided, can be shown or stored in the UI as a reference number (display only, no persistence beyond the current page state).

Workflow 4 – Read FAQs, bookings, terms, and privacy information:
1. User clicks the FAQ, Bookings & Cancellations, Terms of Service, or Privacy links in the internal navigation.
2. section-anchor-navigation updates the URL section parameter via the browser history API (e.g., about.html?section=faqs) without a full reload and scrolls to the relevant section.
3. faqs-summary-section displays faqs_summary from getAboutPageContent and may offer links to a more detailed FAQ page if such navigation exists.
4. bookings-info-section displays explanatory text about bookings, cancellations, and program handling (provided by the server as rendered page content, not hardcoded in the client logic).
5. terms-section and privacy-section display legal text or summaries (again, provided server-side or via CMS integration, not via this page’s APIs), but remain accessible via the same section parameter logic.

Workflow 5 – Navigate back to the home page:
1. User clicks the site logo or a 'Back to Home' link on the About & Info page.
2. The click triggers a standard navigation to index.html.
3. No additional API calls specific to About & Info are required for this navigation; the current page state is not preserved once the user leaves.
interactions: Click internal navigation link (e.g., 'Mission', 'Coaches', 'FAQs', 'Bookings & Cancellations', 'Terms', 'Privacy', 'Contact'): triggers section-anchor-navigation.onClickSectionLink, which updates the URL section parameter (using pushState/replaceState), sets currentSection in about-page-controller, and scrolls to the associated section component.

Change URL section parameter directly (e.g., typing about.html?section=privacy, or following an external deep link): on page load, about-page-controller.onInit reads the parameter, sets currentSection, and after getAboutPageContent resolves, scrolls to the matching section and marks it active in the navigation.

Page load / refresh: triggers about-page-controller.onInit, which calls getAboutPageContent, sets loading and error flags, and distributes the loaded data (mission, philosophy, coaches, contact info, FAQs summary) to the relevant components.

Scroll within the page: updates which section is currently visible; optionally, about-page-controller or section-anchor-navigation can listen to scroll events to update currentSection and synchronize it with the URL section parameter (without introducing localStorage).

Fill in contact form fields: contact-form.onFieldChange updates local component state for name, email, phone, subject, and message; form-status-indicator clears previous error messages when the user edits fields.

Submit contact form: contact-form.onSubmit performs client-side validation; on success it calls submitContactInquiry; while awaiting the response, the form enters a pending state (submit button disabled, optional inline loader); results are communicated to form-status-indicator.
Click logo or 'Back to Home': triggers a standard browser navigation to index.html; no special page state is carried over except what is encoded in the target URL (if any).
page_functionality.state_logic: The page state is controlled primarily by the URL section parameter and the results of getAboutPageContent and submitContactInquiry. On initialization, about-page-controller reads window.location.search to extract the optional section parameter. Valid known section keys (e.g., 'mission', 'philosophy', 'coaches', 'faqs', 'bookings', 'terms', 'privacy', 'contact') are mapped to internal section IDs used by section-anchor-navigation and each content section component. If section is missing or not one of the recognized keys, currentSection defaults to a neutral state (e.g., no active highlight, or the first section). After getAboutPageContent completes successfully, about-page-controller triggers a scroll to the DOM element corresponding to currentSection, if any. When the user clicks an internal navigation link, section-anchor-navigation updates the URL with the new section parameter (using pushState or a standard navigation) and notifies about-page-controller to set currentSection accordingly and scroll into view; no localStorage is used. The getAboutPageContent API is called once per page load (or on hard navigation), and its returned object drives derived UI state: mission, philosophy, coaches, contact_email, contact_phone, and faqs_summary. Loading and error states are stored in memory in about-page-controller to control what each section displays (e.g., loading placeholders or error messages). The contact-form maintains transient field values and validation state in memory; on successful submitContactInquiry, it may clear its local fields and set a success state that form-status-indicator displays. The submitContactInquiry response (success flag, inquiry_id, and message) is used only for immediate UI feedback; it is not persisted beyond the current page view and is not stored in URL parameters.

components.functionality: Coordinates data loading for the About & Info page, parses the URL section parameter, manages currentSection, and passes getAboutPageContent data to child components.
data_binding: ContactInquiry (via submitContactInquiry response for display only)

components.functionality: Renders internal navigation links to each main section (mission, philosophy, coaches, FAQs, bookings info, terms, privacy, contact) and synchronizes the active section with the URL section parameter and scroll position.
Displays the consultancy’s mission text, including loading and error states, using the mission value from getAboutPageContent.
Displays the consultancy’s philosophy/approach text, including loading and error states, using the philosophy value from getAboutPageContent.

Shows a list of key coaches/practitioners with their name, role, and short bio using the coaches array from getAboutPageContent, optionally allowing simple interactions such as expanding a coach entry to show full text if provided.
Displays a brief FAQ summary or overview using faqs_summary from getAboutPageContent, and may expose links/anchors to a fuller FAQ resource if present elsewhere on the site.

Displays explanatory information about how bookings, cancellations, and programs are handled. The text content is provided via server-rendered HTML or CMS configuration (not hardcoded in client logic) but is exposed as a dedicated section that can be scrolled to via the section parameter.
data_binding: Server-rendered content for bookings/cancellations/program handling (no additional client API)

components.functionality: Displays terms of service/legal information for transparency. Content is supplied by the server or CMS; this component primarily provides an anchor target and structure for navigation via the section parameter.

Displays privacy information describing how user data (including bookings and contact inquiries) is handled. Content is provided by the server or CMS and can be accessed directly via the section parameter.
Shows the primary contact email and phone number retrieved from getAboutPageContent, so users can contact the consultancy directly without using the form if they prefer.
Captures user inquiries with fields for name, email, phone, subject, and message; validates inputs and sends them via submitContactInquiry, then reports the response to the user.
data_binding: ContactInquiry (fields: name, email, phone, subject, message)

components.functionality: Displays inline feedback for the contact-form, including validation errors, pending state, success confirmation, and API error messages, based on the outcome of submitContactInquiry.
Provides a simple way for the user to navigate back to the home page (index.html), consistent with the site logo behavior.
data_binding: Static route to index.html (no dynamic data required)

layout.grid_system: Visually consistent with a 12-column responsive grid centered in the viewport, with a max-width content container and substantial left/right margins.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows a linear, top-to-bottom flow under the fixed global header and utility bar. Inside a centered max-width container on a 12-column grid, the top of the About page is dominated by a single, large hero-style mission section on the left, visually echoing the mockup’s bold hero card. To the right of this hero, a slim vertical rail is reserved for the section anchor navigation, inspired by the mockup’s narrow vertical band and control. Below the hero, the rest of the content (philosophy, coaches, FAQs, bookings, legal, contact) is stacked in a single main column, occasionally using internal two-column splits (e.g., coaches grid, legal pair, contact vs form) but always reading as a vertical editorial flow.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows a linear, top-to-bottom flow under the fixed global header and utility bar. Inside a centered max-width container on a 12-column grid, the top of the About page is dominated by a single, large hero-style mission section on the left, visually echoing the mockup’s bold hero card. To the right of this hero, a slim vertical rail is reserved for the section anchor navigation, inspired by the mockup’s narrow vertical band and control. Below the hero, the rest of the content (philosophy, coaches, FAQs, bookings, legal, contact) is stacked in a single main column, occasionally using internal two-column splits (e.g., coaches grid, legal pair, contact vs form) but always reading as a vertical editorial flow.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The fixed utility bar and header occupy a shallow combined band at the very top of the viewport. The main content area begins below this and is centered in a max-width container (approx. 1100–1200px). The hero-style mission section takes roughly 60–70% of the viewport height and about two-thirds to three-quarters of the container width, leaving the remaining rightmost quarter for the vertical anchor navigation rail. Subsequent sections (philosophy, FAQs, bookings) typically span around 70–80% of the container width and are centered, with generous vertical spacing (48–64px) between them. The coaches list and legal sections use more of the horizontal width (up to 100% of the grid) but still maintain wide left/right padding. The final contact band is tall and roomy, occupying a substantial portion of the lower viewport, with a split layout between contact info and form.
layout_strategies.content_density: The layout keeps density low, in line with the mockup: large blocks of whitespace, clear breathing room around cards, and short text groupings. The hero mission card is visually dominant but light on text. Each section below has a clear heading, 1–2 concise paragraphs, and supporting elements (lists, accordions, cards) with comfortable internal padding (24–32px). Denser content like terms and privacy is contained within their own cards and may show summaries or scannable subsections rather than long uninterrupted text, so the overall perception remains spacious and professional.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow starts at the global logo in the fixed header, moves across the centered navigation, then drops into the About page’s main content. Within the page, the back-to-home link and mission hero heading at the top-left are seen first, then the bold blue gradient of the mission hero draws the eye downward. From there, attention moves left-to-right across the hero card and then to the right-side vertical anchor navigation rail, which hints at deeper, scrollable content. As users scroll, the eye continues down through clearly separated sections: philosophy, coaches, FAQs, bookings, legal, and finally the contact section. The anchor navigation remains a subtle fixed/sticky reference on larger screens, supporting an F-shaped reading pattern: top header, hero, then down and across each subsequent block.

layout.overall_description: Beneath a fixed, full-width header and utility bar, the About & Info page sits inside a centered max-width container on a 12-column grid with generous left and right margins. At the very top of the main content, a small, understated back-to-home text link is aligned to the left, followed by a tall, hero-like mission card that occupies roughly the left two-thirds of the content width and 60–70% of the viewport height. This hero card uses the brand’s bold blue gradient, with the consultancy’s mark and a concise mission statement laid out in a spacious, editorial style. Running alongside it on the right is a slim vertical rail: a pale background with a subtle vertical divider and compact anchor links, acting as a modern, sticky section navigator that visually echoes the mockup’s narrow control band. Below this hero zone, the layout transitions into a clean stack of content sections—philosophy, coaches, FAQs, bookings information—each centered and wrapped in lightly elevated cards or clear typographic groupings with ample 48–64px vertical spacing between them. The coaches and legal sections make fuller use of the horizontal grid, introducing structured multi-column content without feeling crowded. At the bottom, a wide, calm contact band stretches across the container, splitting into a left column with contact details and a right column with a spacious contact form card and status feedback. Throughout, the page feels airy, corporate, and confident, guiding the user smoothly from high-level mission to practical information and finally to clear contact and booking pathways.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The about-page-controller wraps the entire About & Info main content area below the fixed global utility bar and header. It establishes a centered max-width content container (approximately 1100–1200px wide) aligned to the middle of the viewport, with generous left and right margins visible on large screens. Inside, it applies a 12-column responsive grid, reserving the top 96–120px of vertical space (including offset from the fixed header) before the first in-page elements appear. All page sections—back-to-home link, mission hero, vertical anchor navigation rail, and subsequent content sections—are arranged vertically within this controller, respecting a consistent vertical rhythm with 48–64px gaps between major blocks. On smaller screens, the controller ensures all sections collapse into a single-column flow with full-width cards and appropriate padding.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static container in normal document flow; defines grid and responsive breakpoints but does not scroll independently or fix itself.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The section-anchor-navigation is positioned in a slim vertical band along the right side of the main content container, starting roughly aligned with the top third of the mission hero card. On desktop, it occupies about 20–25% of the container width but visually reads as a narrow rail (e.g., 220–260px), separated from the main content by a subtle vertical divider line. Its background is a light neutral or very pale tint, and the anchor links are stacked vertically with generous 16–24px spacing, providing quick jumps to Mission, Philosophy, Coaches, FAQs, Bookings, Terms, Privacy, and Contact. This rail begins just to the right of the mission card and continues down, maintaining alignment with the hero’s right edge. As the user scrolls past the hero, the rail becomes sticky within the viewport, hugging the top margin beneath the header to echo the mockup’s persistent right-hand control. On tablet, it shrinks slightly but keeps the vertical format; on mobile, it collapses into a horizontal pill-style bar or a compact dropdown positioned directly under the mission section, still centered within the content width.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sticky within the main content area on medium and large screens, remaining visible along the right edge as the user scrolls; reflows to standard in-flow or collapsible horizontal control on small screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The mission-section is the dominant hero card of the page, positioned near the top of the main content column, immediately below the back-to-home link and page heading area. On desktop, it spans roughly 8 of the 12 grid columns, anchored to the left, occupying about two-thirds to three-quarters of the container width, and stands 60–70% of the viewport height. It features a bold blue-green gradient background with rounded corners and a subtle elevation shadow, echoing the mockup’s full-bleed hero visual. Within the card, content is aligned left with 32px internal padding on all sides: the consultancy’s logo mark or icon in the top-left, a prominent “About Our Consultancy”/mission headline beneath it, followed by 1–2 short paragraphs describing the mission and approach. Optional small supporting elements—like a single key stat or tagline—sit near the bottom-left of the card, leaving ample negative space on the right to keep the hero feeling light. The right edge of this card lines up close to the vertical divider for the anchor navigation, reinforcing the relationship between the hero and the right-side rail.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static hero block in normal flow; height adapts slightly with content but maintains a minimum viewport-relative height on larger screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The philosophy-section is stacked directly beneath the mission hero, separated by a tall band of whitespace (approximately 56–64px). It is centered within the content container and spans around 7–8 of the 12 columns (70–80% width), giving it a more focused, editorial feel compared to the wide hero. The section is presented as a white card with subtle shadow or border, 24–32px internal padding, and a clear heading like “Our Philosophy” at the top-left. Below the heading, 2–3 short paragraphs or bullet points explain the consultancy’s approach to sports performance and wellbeing. The content is left-aligned, with generous line spacing and a comfortable margin below the section to clearly separate it from the coaches section. On smaller screens, it expands to near-full width while maintaining its card styling and generous internal padding.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-width card (within the container) in normal document flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The coaches-summary-list appears directly below the philosophy-section with about 48–56px vertical separation. This section uses a broader, grid-like layout that takes advantage of the full content container width (10–12 grid columns), still centered with consistent outer margins. At the top, a left-aligned heading such as “Our Coaching Team” introduces the section, followed by a brief one-sentence description. The coach summaries are presented as a row of cards in a responsive grid: on large screens, 3 cards per row (each around 3–4 grid columns wide), with 24px gaps between them; on smaller screens, this collapses to 2 cards per row, then 1 per row on mobile. Each coach card has 24px internal padding, a small avatar or placeholder image at the top, the coach’s name in bold, role, and 1–2 lines of specialization, all with ample whitespace to avoid visual clutter. The whole list reads as a single, well-structured band that visually balances the denser legal content later in the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static grid section within normal flow; cards wrap responsively based on available width.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The faqs-summary-section is placed beneath the coaches-summary-list with a generous vertical gap (approximately 56–64px), signaling a shift from people-focused content to practical information. It is centered and spans roughly 7–8 of the 12 columns (70–80% width), similar to the philosophy section, maintaining a cohesive rhythm. The section appears as a white card or grouped block with a heading, such as “FAQs,” at the top-left, followed by a short introductory sentence. Below, FAQs are displayed in a vertical list of 5–7 question rows, each row having a bold question on the left and a brief one-line answer or an accordion-style expand control on the right. Each FAQ row has 16–24px vertical padding and is separated by light dividers, keeping the visual density low while still providing scannable structure. The right-side anchor navigation aligns vertically with this section, allowing users to jump straight here from the rail.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static section within normal flow; FAQ items may expand/collapse but do not affect overall section positioning aside from increasing height.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The bookings-info-section follows the FAQs with approximately 48–56px of vertical spacing, maintaining the editorial stack. It is centered in the container, spanning about 7–8 columns (70–80% width). Visually, it is treated as a slightly more prominent informational band or mini-hero card, with a clean white or very light tinted background and 24–32px internal padding. The heading, such as “Bookings & Sessions,” appears at the top-left, with a short paragraph summarizing how to book consultations or sessions. Below, a concise 2–3 column layout may be used on large screens (e.g., “One-to-One Coaching,” “Team Workshops,” “Corporate Programs”) with each column holding a short description or key bullet points. On smaller screens this naturally stacks into a single column. A subtle text link or button leading users to the contact section or booking form area is aligned towards the bottom-right of the card, tying the section into the eventual contact form call to action.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static card in normal flow; any internal columns collapse responsively to a single-column layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The terms-section appears below the bookings area, forming the left half of a legal information band that spans the full content container width. There is a clear 56–64px vertical gap from the previous section. On desktop, the content container is split into two equal columns: Terms on the left and Privacy on the right, each occupying about 5–6 grid columns. The terms-section is rendered as a white card with defined borders, 24–32px internal padding, and a heading like “Terms & Conditions” at the top-left. The text content is structured into short subsections: a few bolded subheadings with short paragraphs underneath, perhaps truncated with a “View full terms” link if the content is long, to maintain the light visual density. On mobile, this section stacks above the privacy-section, each occupying full width with consistent spacing between them.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within normal flow; pairs with privacy-section in a two-column layout on wider screens, stacks vertically on smaller screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The privacy-section sits directly to the right of the terms-section on large screens, forming the right half of the dual legal band. It mirrors the terms-section in width (about 5–6 grid columns), card style, and padding (24–32px), creating a visually balanced pair. The heading, such as “Privacy Policy,” is aligned top-left, with content below broken into brief, scannable subsections or summaries of key points like data usage, storage, and rights. A “View full privacy policy” link is positioned near the bottom of the card for full detail. On smaller screens, privacy-section appears below the terms-section as a separate full-width card, with a 32–40px gap between the two, preserving the clear separation but maintaining overall consistency with the rest of the stack.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within normal flow; participates in a responsive two-column/stacked pattern with the terms-section.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact-info-block is part of the final, prominent contact band at the bottom of the page. This band appears after a generous 64px gap below the legal sections, signaling a clear shift to action. The contact band spans the full content container width and is internally split into two columns on desktop: the contact-info-block on the left (about 4–5 grid columns, roughly 35–40% of the width) and the contact-form on the right (about 7–8 columns). The contact-info-block is left-aligned, with a heading like “Contact & Location,” followed by key contact details (phone, email, address, operating hours) laid out with 8–16px spacing between lines. Icons or subtle labels help scanability, but the overall presentation is minimal and tidy, with comfortable whitespace around. The block is vertically aligned so that its heading aligns with the form’s heading on the right, and its height is typically shorter than the form, forming a visually stable left anchor for the contact area. On mobile, this block sits above the form, centered, occupying full width with preserved padding and spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within normal flow; forms the left column of a split layout on larger screens and stacks above the contact-form on smaller screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact-form occupies the right side of the final contact band and is treated as a key action card. On desktop, it spans roughly 7–8 of the 12 grid columns (about 60–65% of the content width), aligned to the right, with the left edge lining up with the right edge of the contact-info-block. It has a white background, soft corners, subtle shadow, and generous 24–32px internal padding. At the top-left of the card is a clear heading such as “Send Us a Message” with a brief single-line description beneath. Form fields (name, email, subject, message, etc.) are arranged in a single column with 16–24px vertical space between them to maintain the airy feel. The primary submit button is aligned left or slightly right of center near the bottom, with 8–12px vertical and 16–24px horizontal padding, echoing the button sizing seen in the mockup. The form’s height on desktop is substantial—visually occupying a good portion of the lower viewport—reinforcing its importance. On smaller screens, the form expands to full width beneath the contact-info-block but retains its card styling and internal spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within normal flow; its vertical size increases with validation messages, but it remains anchored as the main CTA zone at the bottom of the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The form-status-indicator is positioned within the contact-form card so that it is immediately visible but does not disrupt the clean layout. On desktop, it appears as a small horizontal status area directly beneath the form heading and description, aligned to the right or full-width just above the first form field. It occupies a shallow band—no more than 1–2 lines of text height—with subtle background coloring (green for success, red for error, neutral for pending) and concise messaging. It respects the form’s internal padding (24–32px from card edges) and has 8–12px vertical margin separating it from the heading above and the first input below. When inactive, this area may be hidden altogether, preserving the minimalist feel of the form. On mobile, it remains in this relative position—just under the heading, spanning the form’s full width—to ensure visibility in a narrower layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline element within the contact-form; appears and disappears dynamically but does not change its relative placement when shown.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The back-to-home-link is a subtle navigational element placed at the very top of the About page’s main content, directly inside the about-page-controller and just below the fixed global header’s bottom edge. It is left-aligned, sitting flush with the main content’s left margin (aligned with the left edge of the mission hero card). The link is small and understated—perhaps an arrow icon followed by “Back to home”—with modest 8–12px vertical padding above and below to keep it clear from the hero card below. There is around 16–24px of space between this link and the top edge of the mission-section, providing a clear but compact separation so the hero remains dominant. On smaller screens, it remains in the same top-left position relative to the content, acting as an easy way to navigate back without competing visually with the main navigation.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline navigation element at the top of the content flow; scrolls away naturally as the user moves down the page.
